name: Ad Hominem
subtitle: Personal Attack
summary: Attacking the person instead of their arguments. One of the oldest logical fallacies.
definition: Ad hominem (Latin for 'against the person') is a logical fallacy in which one attacks the character, motives, or other personal attributes of the person making an argument, rather than addressing the substance of what they are arguing.
historicalContext: Classified by Aristotle among the sophisms. Used systematically by totalitarian regimes: the label 'enemy of the people' delegitimized any argument, however valid.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the source heuristic: we tend to evaluate information based on who is saying it. When the source is delegitimized, the brain automatically discards even valid arguments along with it.

variants.name: Abusive
variants.desc: Direct insults: 'You're an idiot!'

variants.name: Circumstantial
variants.desc: Attacking interests: 'You're paid to say that!'

variants.name: Tu Quoque
variants.desc: Hypocrisy accusation: 'You do the same thing!'

variants.name: Guilt by Association
variants.desc: Guilt by proximity: 'They talk to X, therefore...'

redFlags: The argument focuses on the person making it, not on the proposal itself
Repeated use of negative labels
No engagement with the specific content being argued

caseStudies.title: Galileo
caseStudies.description: Branded a 'heretic' rather than having his astronomical arguments refuted on their merits.
caseStudies.lesson: Personal flaws do not invalidate arguments.

defense.action: Identify when the discussion shifts from facts to the person
Isolate the argument from personal attributes
'What does this have to do with the specific proposal?'

scenario.title: Energy debate
scenario.situation: An engineer proposes nuclear power plants. An opponent: 'We can't listen to him! He works for a company in that sector!'
scenario.question: What is missing from this objection to make it a valid argument?

options.text: No technical aspect of the proposal is challenged — only the professional role of the person advancing it
options.explanation: Conflicts of interest should be declared and weighed, but they do not replace technical rebuttal. A proposal can be valid even when advanced by someone with a stake in the outcome.

options.text: A conflict of interest is sufficient on its own to invalidate an expert's analysis
options.explanation: No — the source must be evaluated, not dismissed. Knowing about an interest helps you read critically; it does not justify automatic rejection.

options.text: The opponent has not responded, which makes the objection technically valid
options.explanation: Silence is not consent. The engineer may have chosen not to respond to an objection that does not merit technical rebuttal.

name: Ridicule
subtitle: The Mocker's Weapon
summary: Turning the opponent into an object of mockery to delegitimize them without arguments.
definition: Systematic ridicule uses humor to make it socially costly to take the opponent seriously.
historicalContext: Used by regimes through caricatures of ethnic groups and dissidents. In the digital age, memes have become powerful weapons.
psychologicalMechanism: Humor creates an emotional association that bypasses reasoning. Those who laugh at someone categorize them as 'not worthy of being taken seriously'.

variants.name: Serial memes
variants.desc: Coordinated campaigns against the same figure

variants.name: Selective edits
variants.desc: Compilations of gaffes taken out of context

variants.name: Derogatory nicknames
variants.desc: Humorous labels that replace the person's name

redFlags: Memes targeting physical appearance rather than ideas
Systematic one-sided satire
Mockery without any argument

caseStudies.title: The power of memes
caseStudies.description: Studies show that candidates systematically ridiculed in memes are perceived as less competent, regardless of their actual positions.
caseStudies.lesson: Memes are political communication with measurable effects.

defense.action: 'It makes me laugh' does not mean 'they are wrong'
Who produces the content that makes you laugh?
How would you react if the same technique were used against 'your side'?

scenario.title: Meme campaign
scenario.situation: For months, social media is flooded with memes showing a politician with a distorted face. There are no arguments, only mockery.
scenario.question: Why is this meme campaign more effective than direct criticism of the politician?

options.text: It associates the politician with mockery before the audience can evaluate their positions on the merits
options.explanation: Once an emotional association is established, it is far harder to dislodge than a rational argument. The politician becomes 'the meme person' before they can even speak.

options.text: An audience that laughs is more open to the arguments that follow
options.explanation: On the contrary: laughter closes critical judgment. Someone who has laughed at a person rarely re-evaluates them as a serious interlocutor.

options.text: Satire always produces a defensive reaction that exposes the target
options.explanation: Context matters. Responding to satire can appear as lacking a sense of humour, not as legitimate defence.

name: Demonization
subtitle: The Absolute Enemy
summary: Representing the opponent as the embodiment of evil, devoid of any human qualities.
definition: Demonization portrays the opponent not as someone with different opinions, but as a force of pure evil, an existential threat, a being without morality or humanity.
historicalContext: A central technique in all genocides: Jews, Tutsi, Armenians were first demonized as 'rats', 'cockroaches', 'parasites'. Demonization psychologically prepares the population for violence.
psychologicalMechanism: Dehumanizes the enemy, removing moral inhibitions against violence. When someone is 'pure evil', any action against them is justified.

variants.name: Subhuman
variants.desc: Comparisons to animals, parasites, diseases

variants.name: Existential threat
variants.desc: 'They want to destroy everything we love'

variants.name: Conspiracy
variants.desc: 'They are everywhere, they control everything'

variants.name: Morally inferior
variants.desc: 'They have no values, they are barbarians'

redFlags: Comparisons to animals or diseases
Representation as an existential threat
Denial of humanity to the opponent
Language implying the need for 'purification' or 'elimination'

caseStudies.title: Radio Rwanda
caseStudies.description: Before the genocide, the Tutsi were called 'inyenzi' (cockroaches). Demonization prepared the population for the massacre.
caseStudies.lesson: Demonization is the first step toward mass violence.

caseStudies.title: Nazi propaganda
caseStudies.description: Jews were represented as rats 'infesting' society. The removal of humanity made the 'Final Solution' possible.
caseStudies.lesson: Words precede actions.

defense.action: Notice when the opponent loses human attributes in discourse
Demonization has preceded every genocide
No group is monolithic or purely evil
Do not use or accept terms that dehumanize

scenario.title: Public speech
scenario.situation: A speaker: 'These parasites won't stop until they've destroyed everything we love. They are a plague that must be eradicated.'
scenario.question: What function does the animal and parasitic language in this speech serve?

options.text: It removes the category 'opponent to engage with' and replaces it with 'threat to be eliminated'
options.explanation: Dehumanisation is not merely rhetoric: it psychologically prepares the audience to accept measures that would be unacceptable if applied to persons.

options.text: It expresses a genuine concern with emotional emphasis, which is acceptable in public discourse
options.explanation: Emphasis is acceptable; dehumanisation is not. The difference is that the first amplifies an argument; the second replaces it.

options.text: It motivates the audience through an appeal to patriotism and defence of the community
options.explanation: Motivation is a secondary effect. The primary goal is to redefine the target group as non-human, removing any moral constraint.

name: Firehose of Falsehood
subtitle: Flood of Lies
summary: Flooding the information space with such a volume of contradictory messages that fact-checking becomes impossible.
definition: A propaganda model that bombards the public with many false and contradictory messages — betting that corrections cannot keep pace.
historicalContext: Theorized by the RAND Corporation in 2016 for Russian operations during the annexation of Crimea. Unlike Soviet propaganda, it accepts obvious contradictions.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the 'illusory truth effect' (repetition makes things seem true) and 'cognitive fatigue' (too many contradictory facts = I stop verifying).

variants.name: High volume
variants.desc: More information than can be verified

variants.name: Speed
variants.desc: Rapid publication before responses can be mounted

variants.name: Incoherence
variants.desc: Accepts obvious contradictions

variants.name: Multiple channels
variants.desc: Different platforms simultaneously

redFlags: Constant flow of news that contradicts previous narratives
Stories that rapidly change version
Contradictory narratives in the same space

caseStudies.title: MH17
caseStudies.description: After the shootdown, 15 different explanations were proposed within days: Ukrainian missile, fighter jet, CIA plot...
caseStudies.lesson: A plurality of explanations can be more effective than a single lie.

defense.action: Do not react in real time
Seek confirmation from independent sources
Recognize multiple and contradictory narratives

scenario.title: International crisis
scenario.situation: Within 48 hours: 'There are no troops' (Mon), 'Defensive exercises' (Tue morning), 'The enemy provoked us' (Tue afternoon), 'We were attacked' (Tue evening).
scenario.question: What is the real objective of this sequence of contradictory statements?

options.text: To make it impossible to establish a definitive version of events — confusion is the product, not a side effect
options.explanation: When narratives contradict each other fast enough, the audience stops searching for truth and develops generalised cynicism. Total doubt is more useful than a single credible lie.

options.text: To update the position as the situation develops: rapid changes are normal in a crisis
options.explanation: Legitimate updates have a direction and logic. Here the contradictions occur within hours, without new facts to justify them.

options.text: To confuse the enemy with false information to protect tactical positions
options.explanation: That is the military purpose. Here the target is domestic and international public opinion, not the enemy on the battlefield.

name: Chain Letter
subtitle: Viral Amplification
summary: Exploiting virality to amplify unverified or false information.
definition: The modern chain letter exploits social media algorithms to amplify emotionally charged content, regardless of its truthfulness.
historicalContext: Born as paper letter chains, it evolved with social media. Algorithms reward engagement, not truth.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits social proof and emotion: content that generates anger or fear spreads faster.

variants.name: Emotional chain
variants.desc: 'Share if you have a heart!'

variants.name: Alarm
variants.desc: 'Urgent warning for everyone!'

variants.name: Luck chain
variants.desc: 'Share or something bad will happen to you'

variants.name: Viral petition
variants.desc: Fake or manipulated petitions

redFlags: Explicit call to share
Highly emotional content without sources
Threats or promises tied to sharing

caseStudies.title: WhatsApp chains
caseStudies.description: During epidemics and elections, unverified message chains spread exponentially, bypassing fact-checkers.
caseStudies.lesson: The speed of sharing outpaces the speed of verification.

defense.action: Do not forward automatically
Look for independent sources
Reply with a fact-check if possible

scenario.title: Viral message
scenario.situation: You receive: 'URGENT! New banking scam! Share with all your contacts to protect them!' No sources.
scenario.question: Which element of this message should stop you before you share it?

options.text: The urgency combined with the absence of verifiable sources — messages designed to spread fast often are because they would not survive slow scrutiny
options.explanation: Urgency is a mechanism for bypassing critical thinking. A genuinely important alert can wait the 60 seconds needed to verify it.

options.text: The absence of an institutional signature — but that alone does not prove the content is false
options.explanation: Correct that it does not prove falsity, but an institutional signature is one verification criterion among several. The absence of sources is more decisive.

options.text: The banking topic is too technical to be communicated in this format
options.explanation: The simplicity of the format is not the problem. Much useful information is simple. The problem is the structure of the message, not the complexity of the topic.

name: Framing
subtitle: Narrative Frame
summary: The facts don't change — only the frame through which they are presented.
definition: Framing selects certain aspects of a reality and makes them more salient, promoting a particular problem definition, causal interpretation, or moral evaluation.
historicalContext: Concept derived from media sociology (Goffman, Entman). The same facts can be presented through completely different frames.
psychologicalMechanism: Operates through accessibility (what is presented first influences interpretation) and applicability (the frame activates certain mental schemas).

variants.name: Conflict frame
variants.desc: Presenting as a clash between sides

variants.name: Economic frame
variants.desc: Evaluating in terms of costs/benefits

variants.name: Moral frame
variants.desc: Questions of values, good/evil

variants.name: Security frame
variants.desc: Presenting as a threat

redFlags: Same event described with very different words by different sources
Systematically emotionally charged terms
Selective focus on certain aspects

caseStudies.title: Immigration
caseStudies.description: Outlet A: 'security crisis' (invasion, illegals). Outlet B: 'humanitarian emergency' (refugees, hope).
caseStudies.lesson: No need to lie: the selection constructs different realities.

defense.action: How is the event being presented?
Are the terms neutral or loaded?
How is it being presented elsewhere?

scenario.title: Street protest
scenario.situation: 10,000 people protest, 50 cause disturbances. Outlet A: 'VIOLENCE IN THE CITY'. Outlet B: 'TEN THOUSAND IN THE STREETS'.
scenario.question: Both outlets report verifiable facts. How do they generate opposite perceptions?

options.text: The choice of which detail becomes the headline determines which interpretive frame the reader takes home — the facts are the same, the frames are not
options.explanation: Framing does not invent facts: it selects which fact becomes figure and which becomes background. 50 violent protesters are a real datum, as are 10,000 peaceful marchers — but the headline chooses which defines the event.

options.text: Outlet A has more resources and can do more complete, detailed reporting
options.explanation: Having more resources does not imply more accurate framing — it may mean a more elaborate and persuasive narrative, for better or worse.

options.text: Outlet B is biased because it minimises the violence with its numerical framing
options.explanation: Mentioning the 10,000 does not minimise the 50: it provides proportional context. It is Outlet A that loses context, not Outlet B that distorts facts.

name: Whataboutism
subtitle: The Deflection
summary: Deflecting every criticism by pointing to others' wrongdoings instead of responding.
definition: A variant of the 'tu quoque' fallacy: instead of responding to a criticism, the accused accuses the critic of the same behavior.
historicalContext: Perfected by Soviet propaganda. When the West criticized human rights in the USSR, the response was: 'And you? You have racism!'
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the desire for moral consistency: we feel uncomfortable criticizing someone if we ourselves are not perfect.

variants.name: Direct whatabout
variants.desc: 'What about you? You do the same thing!'

variants.name: Historical whatabout
variants.desc: 'And what about you in 1950?'

variants.name: Systemic whatabout
variants.desc: 'And what about capitalism?'

variants.name: Selective whatabout
variants.desc: Only cases that favor one's own narrative

redFlags: A response that doesn't address the question
'Yes, but...' followed by someone else's faults
Comparisons used to justify anything

caseStudies.title: Soviet responses
caseStudies.description: Criticism of treatment of dissidents answered with: 'And Black people in America?' Legitimate critiques used to shut down debate.
caseStudies.lesson: Valid criticisms can be used as shields to deflect other valid criticisms.

defense.action: 'Did you answer the question, or did you ask a different one?'
'Can we talk about the two things separately?'
'Now: is this decision right or wrong?'

scenario.title: Difficult interview
scenario.situation: Journalist: 'Your party was convicted of illegal financing.' Politician: 'And party X? They did worse!'
scenario.question: What practical effect does the politician achieve with this response?

options.text: Shifts the plane of debate without answering the specific question — the journalist must now choose whether to chase the new topic or insist on the original
options.explanation: The move works because it puts the journalist in an uncomfortable position: insisting looks like harassment; letting go means losing the thread.

options.text: Demonstrates that the accusation is unfounded by bringing a comparable case
options.explanation: A comparable case does not invalidate the original accusation. Two wrongs do not make a right: the conviction stands regardless.

options.text: Rebalances the conversation by highlighting the journalist's hypocrisy
options.explanation: The journalist is not accused of anything in this conversation. 'Others do it too' is deflection, not rebalancing.

name: Agenda Setting
subtitle: Control of the Invisible
summary: It's not important how we talk about it, but WHAT we don't talk about.
definition: The ability of media to influence not what the public thinks, but what the public thinks about.
historicalContext: Theory formulated by McCombs and Shaw in 1972: the media don't tell us what to think, but what to think about.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain assesses relevance based on media salience. If something is widely discussed, it must be important.

variants.name: Selective amplification
variants.desc: Some topics receive constant coverage, others none

variants.name: Selective timing
variants.desc: Inconvenient news published at low-attention moments

variants.name: Gatekeeping
variants.desc: Simply not allowing certain topics through

variants.name: Competitive agenda
variants.desc: Saturating with irrelevant topics

redFlags: Scandals with disproportionate coverage
Important topics with zero attention
News 'buried' at minor moments

caseStudies.title: Saturation
caseStudies.description: During weeks of scandal coverage, important legislation passes unnoticed.
caseStudies.lesson: What is not said is more important than what is said.

defense.action: 'What is NOT being talked about?'
Different outlets have different agendas
Read minor sections too

scenario.title: Media week
scenario.situation: For a week, 15 of 20 minutes devoted to a celebrity accused of fraud. An approved electoral reform gets 30 seconds late at night.
scenario.question: What impact does this distribution of airtime have on citizens' ability to vote in an informed way?

options.text: Citizens did not receive the information necessary to evaluate a reform that directly affects them
options.explanation: The media agenda does not only decide what is interesting: it decides what exists in the public debate. An unknown reform is one on which no opinion is formed.

options.text: It reflects audience preferences: the celebrity story was what people wanted to hear
options.explanation: Circularity: the public prefers what it is exposed to. Audience preferences are partly constructed by the agenda itself.

options.text: It is a legitimate editorial choice: news stories have different weight and editors must choose
options.explanation: Editorial discretion is real but not neutral. The political consequences of this choice are documented and serious.

name: Poisoning the Well
subtitle: Preemptive Discrediting
summary: Pre-judging a source before they can speak, making it impossible to receive their arguments.
definition: Presenting negative information about a source BEFORE they can express themselves, so that whatever they say is filtered through that negative lens.
historicalContext: The term comes from the ancient practice of literally poisoning wells. In rhetoric, it is a preemptive form of attack.
psychologicalMechanism: The primacy of information: the first information received about someone influences the interpretation of everything that follows.

variants.name: Pre-defamation
variants.desc: Revealing 'negative information' before a speech

variants.name: Preemptive association
variants.desc: 'They're about to speak, but remember that...'

variants.name: Anticipatory disqualification
variants.desc: 'Everything they say will be false because...'

redFlags: Negative information presented before the source speaks
Warnings about 'don't trust X' before events
Systematic negative qualifiers

caseStudies.title: Political debates
caseStudies.description: Before a debate, negative news about a candidate is spread, so every word they say is viewed with suspicion.
caseStudies.lesson: Preemptive damage is hard to repair.

defense.action: Is the negative information arriving BEFORE they speak?
Evaluate the arguments, not just the source
Is the preemptively spread information actually true?

scenario.title: Pre-event
scenario.situation: Before an expert's conference, this circulates: 'Warning: they have ties to interested industries!' No specifics on which, or whether it affects their research.
scenario.question: What makes this warning potentially manipulative, even if its content were accurate?

options.text: It installs a filter of suspicion before the audience can evaluate the argument autonomously
options.explanation: Sequence matters: informing after listening allows evaluation; informing before listening predetermines it. The effect is the same as a verdict before the trial.

options.text: It is information the audience has a right to know before evaluating a source
options.explanation: True in principle — but here the information is vague ('ties to industries') and does not specify what influence it would have on the research. Intentional vagueness amplifies suspicion without allowing it to be verified.

options.text: It proves the source is unreliable, which automatically invalidates their conclusions
options.explanation: A source with interests can produce valid research. Verification must concern method and data, not only the source.

name: Card Stacking
subtitle: Stacking the Deck
summary: Presenting only the facts that favor one's thesis, hiding the contrary ones.
definition: Building an entire message from true but selectively chosen facts, presented in a one-sided manner.
historicalContext: Term from the Institute for Propaganda Analysis (1937). Differs from lying: the facts are true, but incomplete.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits confirmation bias and the illusion of completeness: if we see a lot of data, we assume the picture is complete.

variants.name: Omission
variants.desc: Simply not mentioning contrary facts

variants.name: Selective emphasis
variants.desc: Giving much space to favorable facts, little or none to contrary ones

variants.name: Missing context
variants.desc: True facts without the context that changes their meaning

redFlags: Very one-sided presentation
Absence of data that might contradict
Sources all from the same orientation

caseStudies.title: Corporate reports
caseStudies.description: Annual reports that celebrate successes but don't mention failures or risks.
caseStudies.lesson: Completeness requires including what we don't like too.

defense.action: 'What data contradicts this thesis?'
What do those with different interests say?
What is NOT being said?

scenario.title: Sales report
scenario.situation: A company: 'We sold 1 million units!' Does not mention that 3 million were projected.
scenario.question: Is the figure cited by the company false?

options.text: No — it is verifiably true, but the omitted context reverses its actual meaning
options.explanation: This is the critical distinction between a lie and manipulation: the figure survives fact-checking, but produces an impression opposite to reality. It is harder to refute than a direct falsehood.

options.text: Yes — presenting a figure without relevant context is equivalent to a false statement
options.explanation: Ethically it may be. But legally and practically the distinction matters: rebuttals work on falsehoods, not on omissions. Debunking this requires completing the picture, not contradicting the figure.

options.text: It cannot be determined without seeing the full press release
options.explanation: We already know that '1 million out of 3 million projected' means the opposite of '1 million in absolute terms'. The problem is the missing frame, not a hidden datum.

name: Labeling
subtitle: Name-Calling
summary: Using labels to define people, groups, or ideas — simplifying and often delegitimizing them.
definition: Applying labels (positive or negative) that substitute for argumentation and block critical thinking.
historicalContext: Used systematically in every conflict: 'terrorist' vs 'freedom fighter', 'patriot' vs 'nationalist'.
psychologicalMechanism: Labels activate stereotypes and bypass analysis. Once a label is applied, we no longer analyze the content.

variants.name: Demonizing labels
variants.desc: 'Traitor', 'enemy', 'saboteur'

variants.name: Ennobling labels
variants.desc: 'Hero', 'pioneer', 'defender'

variants.name: Reductive labels
variants.desc: 'Right-wing', 'left-wing' to shut down debate

redFlags: Labels used instead of arguments
Labels that substitute for analysis
Systematic labels for certain groups

caseStudies.title: Cold War
caseStudies.description: 'Communist' was used to delegitimize any progressive position without discussing its merits.
caseStudies.lesson: Labels shut down debate.

defense.action: 'Is that a label or an argument?'
'What does this label specifically mean in this context?'
Reality is more complex than labels

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: Someone proposes a tax reform. The opponent: 'That's typical socialism!' Does not discuss the proposal.
scenario.question: What does the opponent gain by using an ideological label instead of a critique?

options.text: They activate identity-based reactions in the audience without engaging with the proposal's content — those who dislike 'socialists' will reject it, those who like them will support it, without reading a word
options.explanation: The label bypasses reasoning by activating identity. Once a proposal is pigeonholed, it is evaluated based on the label, not the content.

options.text: They provide useful information about the proposer's ideological orientation
options.explanation: Only if the label were accurate and relevant. Here the specific proposal is not analysed, so we cannot know whether the label is correct or instrumental.

options.text: They save time: labels are an efficient tool for categorising political proposals
options.explanation: Efficiency is a virtue only if it does not sacrifice accuracy. Here analysis is replaced by categorisation — the result is faster but less informed.

name: Bandwagon
subtitle: Herd Effect
summary: 'Everyone is doing it!' — Exploiting the desire for social conformity.
definition: Technique that exploits social proof: if 'everyone' does or thinks something, it must be right/true/desirable.
historicalContext: One of the seven main techniques identified by the Institute for Propaganda Analysis in 1937. Used in advertising and politics.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the need for belonging and the fear of being excluded. We tend to conform to what we perceive as social norms.

variants.name: Explicit bandwagon
variants.desc: 'Everyone is doing it!', 'Millions of people...'

variants.name: Implicit bandwagon
variants.desc: Showing cheering crowds, queues at stores

variants.name: False consensus
variants.desc: Inflating support numbers

redFlags: Invitations to join the majority
Unverifiable support numbers
Implicit social pressure

caseStudies.title: The 1936 election
caseStudies.description: The 'Literary Digest' predicted a Landon victory based on millions of questionnaires — but the sample was skewed. The bandwagon proved wrong.
caseStudies.lesson: The majority can be wrong.

defense.action: Is the 'everyone' real or imaginary?
Even if everyone did it, would it be right?
The majority has often supported wrong things

scenario.title: Advertising campaign
scenario.situation: 'Used by millions of people!' Doesn't specify how many millions, or whether it works.
scenario.question: Why is this slogan persuasive despite containing no verifiable information?

options.text: It exploits the tendency to conform to perceived norms: if 'everyone' uses something, the brain interprets it as a quality signal without seeking proof
options.explanation: The evolutionary mechanism is real: in most situations, what everyone does is a reasonable safety signal. Manipulators exploit precisely this shortcut.

options.text: A large number suggests widespread satisfaction, which is indirect evidence of quality
options.explanation: Only if 'millions' were a precise and verifiable number, and if satisfaction were measured. The vague 'millions' is constructed to suggest this without allowing verification.

options.text: It is not persuasive: without evidence, informed audiences do not trust vague numbers
options.explanation: Unfortunately not. Decades of cognitive research show that social proof works even on informed and sceptical people in other areas.

name: Glittering Generalities
subtitle: Shining Words
summary: Using words with positive connotations but no specific meaning.
definition: Words like 'freedom', 'democracy', 'progress', 'justice' used without defining their content, to associate positive feelings with a cause.
historicalContext: Identified by the Institute for Propaganda Analysis. Complementary to name-calling: positive empty words instead of negative ones.
psychologicalMechanism: Positive words activate positive emotions without engaging critical thinking. Who isn't 'for freedom'?

variants.name: Abstract values
variants.desc: 'Freedom', 'justice', 'progress'

variants.name: Empty qualifiers
variants.desc: 'The right solution', 'real change'

variants.name: Positive adjectives
variants.desc: 'Courageous', 'visionary', 'authentic'

redFlags: Words with strong emotional charge but no definition
Everyone can agree but means something different
Statements that sound good but say nothing

caseStudies.title: Election campaigns
caseStudies.description: 'For freedom!', 'For real progress!' Without defining what these words mean in the platform.
caseStudies.lesson: Everyone loves freedom, but defines it differently.

defense.action: 'What does freedom specifically mean in this program?'
Behind the glittering word, what is actually being proposed?
What is NOT being said?

scenario.title: Political slogan
scenario.situation: 'Vote for real freedom and real progress!' No explanation of what 'real' means or what concrete policies are proposed.
scenario.question: Why are terms like 'true freedom' and 'true progress' effective in political communication?

options.text: Everyone projects their preferred meaning onto them: they create apparent consensus on content that has not been defined and that different people understand in opposite ways
options.explanation: This is Orwell's mechanism of 'shield words': terms vague enough to be uncontestable, charged enough to evoke emotional assent.

options.text: They communicate the candidate's value orientation, which is politically relevant information
options.explanation: Only if values were operationalised into proposals. Without content, declared values say nothing about what the candidate will actually do.

options.text: They are only effective during a campaign: voters then judge on performance
options.explanation: This is a hope, not the reality. Research shows that emotional associations established during campaigns persist long after, even in the face of concrete performance.

name: Transfer
subtitle: Authority Transfer
summary: Transferring the authority or prestige of a figure/symbol to a cause.
definition: Associating a cause with respected (or hated) symbols, people, or institutions to automatically transfer those feelings.
historicalContext: Used systematically in advertising (celebrity endorsement) and politics (photographs with flags, religious figures, military).
psychologicalMechanism: The brain automatically transfers feelings from the source to the associated object. If a respected person supports X, X acquires respectability.

variants.name: Religious authority
variants.desc: Associating causes with religious figures or sacred symbols

variants.name: Scientific authority
variants.desc: Citing experts outside their field

variants.name: National symbols
variants.desc: Flags, anthems, monuments to legitimize causes

variants.name: Moral authority
variants.desc: Citing respected historical figures

redFlags: Association with authoritative figures out of context
Use of sacred or national symbols for ordinary causes
Quotes from experts outside their field

caseStudies.title: Scientific advertising
caseStudies.description: An actor who plays a doctor on TV recommends a drug. The authority transfer is fictional but effective.
caseStudies.lesson: Authority must be relevant and real.

defense.action: Does the authority have expertise ON THIS topic?
Does the cause have its own merits, independent of who supports it?
Are they using a symbol to legitimize something unrelated?

scenario.title: Endorsement
scenario.situation: A famous actor supports a position on global economics. They have no economics background.
scenario.question: Why does a famous actor's support for an economic position not constitute an argument?

options.text: Credibility in one domain does not automatically extend to another — excelling at acting does not imply expertise in macroeconomics
options.explanation: The brain generalises credibility ('good at everything') — this is the heuristic being exploited. But competence is specific: an excellent surgeon is not necessarily a good economist, and vice versa.

options.text: The audience can evaluate their arguments autonomously if they articulate them
options.explanation: In theory yes. In practice, the actor rarely argues: they 'support'. The social weight of their endorsement precedes any analysis of the merits.

options.text: Public fame implies responsibility: their words carry more weight and therefore more value
options.explanation: The impact is real but social, not epistemic. Something can have great effect on public opinion and be entirely without argumentative value.

name: Testimonial
subtitle: Personal Testimony
summary: Using personal testimonies instead of data or arguments.
definition: Presenting individual experiences as general proof, or using paid/coached witnesses to reinforce a message.
historicalContext: The foundation of modern advertising. In propaganda, used to humanize causes or delegitimize opponents.
psychologicalMechanism: Personal stories are more persuasive than abstract data. The brain remembers narratives better than statistics.

variants.name: Emotional testimony
variants.desc: Tearjerking stories that bypass data

variants.name: Authority testimony
variants.desc: Celebrities or experts outside their field

variants.name: Orchestrated testimony
variants.desc: Prepared or paid witnesses

redFlags: Emotional stories without data
One experience presented as the norm
Witnesses with hidden interests

caseStudies.title: 'Mary the housewife'
caseStudies.description: Advertising with 'real consumers' who turn out to be actors. The testimony appears spontaneous but is orchestrated.
caseStudies.lesson: Always verify the authenticity and interests of the witness.

defense.action: What do the statistics say, not just the stories?
Do they have interests? Are they representative?
Anecdote is not data; a story is not a statistic

scenario.title: Health campaign
scenario.situation: 'My cousin took this drug and was cured!' Presented as proof of efficacy, without clinical studies.
scenario.question: Why can this individual story not prove the drug's effectiveness?

options.text: A single case cannot be distinguished from spontaneous remission, the placebo effect, or temporal coincidence
options.explanation: Medicine developed controlled clinical trials precisely because anecdotes — even sincere ones — do not allow isolation of the causal variable. There is no bad faith: the cognitive mechanism is universal.

options.text: Real patients' experiences are more reliable than laboratory studies because they come from real people
options.explanation: This is the opposite of the correct intuition. Studies exist to compensate for the fact that individual experiences, however real, do not control for other variables.

options.text: If the person recovered, the drug works — it is the most direct evidence possible
options.explanation: Many illnesses resolve spontaneously. The question is not 'did the patient recover?' but 'did they recover more often than those who did not take the drug?' — and that requires a sample.

name: Repetition
subtitle: Say It Again and Again
summary: Repeating a message until it is accepted as true.
definition: The constant repetition of a statement makes it seem true, regardless of its actual veracity.
historicalContext: 'A lie repeated a thousand times becomes truth' (attributed to Goebbels). Effect studied in psychology as the 'illusory truth effect'.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain uses ease of processing as an indicator of truth. If something is familiar, it seems true.

variants.name: Explicit repetition
variants.desc: The same slogan repeated obsessively

variants.name: Multi-channel repetition
variants.desc: The same message across multiple platforms

variants.name: Repetition with variations
variants.desc: The same concept formulated in different ways

redFlags: The same message everywhere
Slogans repeated without argument
Claims that become 'established facts' without evidence

caseStudies.title: Illusory truth
caseStudies.description: Studies show that statements presented multiple times are judged as truer, even by people who had already classified them as false.
caseStudies.lesson: Familiarity is not truth.

defense.action: Have I heard this many times?
Familiarity is not proof
A lie repeated is still a lie

scenario.title: Ubiquitous slogan
scenario.situation: For months, every channel repeats: 'The problem is X!' No data is presented, but everyone 'knows' it's true.
scenario.question: What psychological mechanism makes a repeated claim feel true, regardless of the facts?

options.text: Familiarity is processed by the brain as a truth signal: processing something with ease (because we've heard it before) is confused with recognising something as true
options.explanation: This is the documented 'illusory truth effect' (Hasher, Goldstein, Toppino, 1977). It works even when people are warned in advance that the content may be false.

options.text: If many different sources say it, they have formed their views independently and agree
options.explanation: In an interconnected media environment, sources often cite each other or share the same original source. Multiplication of voices does not equal independence of sources.

options.text: Repetition is only problematic if the content is false: it depends on the facts
options.explanation: The illusory truth effect works regardless of the content's veracity. Repeating true content is not neutral: it makes it feel 'more true' than other equally true content that is less repeated.

name: Slogan
subtitle: Catchphrase
summary: Short, memorable phrases that substitute for complex thought.
definition: Short, easily remembered and repeated formulas that summarize (and often excessively simplify) a position.
historicalContext: 'Make America Great Again', 'Hope and Change', 'Yes We Can'. Slogans are central to every political campaign.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain prefers information that is easy to process. Slogans are memorized automatically and recalled easily.

variants.name: Identity slogan
variants.desc: '[Country] First!', 'Citizens, not subjects'

variants.name: Action slogan
variants.desc: 'Enough [X]!', 'Yes we can!'

variants.name: Contrastive slogan
variants.desc: 'They destroy, we build'

redFlags: Very short phrases that summarize complex issues
Easy to shout, impossible to debate
Substitute for arguments

caseStudies.title: Slogan in dictatorship
caseStudies.description: 'One people, one empire, one leader' — summarizes and simplifies a complex totalitarian ideology into three words.
caseStudies.lesson: Slogans bypass critical thinking.

defense.action: 'What does this slogan mean concretely?'
Behind the simple phrase, what is the actual program?
What does the slogan NOT say?

scenario.title: Rally
scenario.situation: The candidate shouts: 'Law and order!' The crowd cheers. There is no discussion of which laws or what kind of order.
scenario.question: What does the slogan sacrifice compared to an articulated policy programme?

options.text: Any specificity: 'law and order' can mean opposite things and find equal resonance among people with incompatible values
options.explanation: This is the strategic utility of the slogan: it is vague enough to unite constituencies that could not coexist on concrete proposals. Those who vote 'law and order' do not know what they are voting for.

options.text: Only length: essential content can be communicated briefly without information loss
options.explanation: Length is not the problem. The problem is that the slogan's keywords do not map onto verifiable policies — brevity conceals this absence, it does not resolve it.

options.text: Credibility: slogans only work with already-convinced supporters
options.explanation: On the contrary: framing research shows slogans are effective even on those not predisposed, as long as the words carry positive emotional valence.

name: Plain Folks
subtitle: One of You
summary: Presenting oneself as 'one of you' to gain trust.
definition: Technique in which those holding power present themselves as ordinary people to build identification and trust.
historicalContext: Identified by the Institute for Propaganda Analysis in 1937. Used by political leaders throughout history.
psychologicalMechanism: We tend to trust those we perceive as similar to us. If the leader is 'like us', they must have our interests at heart.

variants.name: Staged scenes
variants.desc: Politicians who 'go grocery shopping' only during election campaigns

variants.name: Artificial language
variants.desc: Intentional use of colloquial terms by educated people

variants.name: Humble origins
variants.desc: Emphasis on 'modest' origins even when long since past

redFlags: Politicians appearing in 'everyday' contexts only during campaigns
Language studied to seem colloquial
Emphasis on humble origins that don't reflect current reality

caseStudies.title: Joe the Plumber
caseStudies.description: Became a symbol of the common citizen. Investigations showed he wasn't a plumber and his question was prepared.
caseStudies.lesson: Even real people can be orchestrated tools.

defense.action: Is the 'ordinariness' real or performed?
What have they done, not just what do they appear to be?
Is the 'everyday' context spontaneous or arranged?

scenario.title: Viral video
scenario.situation: A video shows 'Maria, 42, housewife' complaining about the government. Filmed professionally, no surname, no city.
scenario.question: Which production detail should cast doubt on the authenticity of this testimonial?

options.text: The professional production quality combined with the absence of any verifiable identifiers — the two together contradict the spontaneity the format implies
options.explanation: An authentic spontaneous testimonial is either filmed amateurishly, or includes verifiable details. Here production is polished and every element that would permit verification is absent.

options.text: The first name: ordinary people may want to protect their privacy with a pseudonym
options.explanation: The name is one criterion, not the only one. But the combination of no surname, no city, and professional video quality is the relevant signal.

options.text: The topic: problems with the government interest everyone, including housewives
options.explanation: The topic is irrelevant for assessing authenticity. The problem is not what Maria says but who produced the video and with what purpose.

name: Appeal to Authority
subtitle: Argument from Authority
summary: Citing authorities as proof, even when they are not relevant or competent on the topic.
definition: A fallacy that cites an authority as definitive proof, even if that authority has no specific competence on the topic.
historicalContext: Aristotle classified the appeal to authority as a fallacy when the authority is not relevant to the subject.
psychologicalMechanism: Respect for authority is culturally internalized. When an authority speaks, we tend to accept without verifying.

variants.name: Authority outside their field
variants.desc: A doctor speaking about economics

variants.name: Compromised authority
variants.desc: Citing sources with hidden interests

variants.name: Lone authority
variants.desc: A single voice against the scientific consensus

redFlags: Experts cited outside their field
A single authority against the consensus
Authorities with hidden conflicts of interest

caseStudies.title: Tobacco and doctors
caseStudies.description: For decades, the industry cited doctors who downplayed the risks of smoking. They were paid.
caseStudies.lesson: Verify the competence, relevance, and interests of the authority.

defense.action: Do they have expertise ON THIS topic?
Is it an isolated voice or the consensus?
Do they have conflicts of interest?

scenario.title: Campaign
scenario.situation: '9 out of 10 scientists say X is safe!' Doesn't specify which scientists, whether they have competence, or interests.
scenario.question: What is missing to make this claim verifiable?

options.text: Identity of the experts, their field of competence specific to this topic, potential conflicts of interest, and how the sample was defined
options.explanation: Without these elements, 'scientists' is an empty category. The same formulation was used by the tobacco industry with paid doctors declaring cigarettes safe.

options.text: A higher number: '9 out of 10' is already an overwhelming majority that is convincing in itself
options.explanation: A sample of 10 scientists selected with a conflict of interest is worth less than a sample of 3 independent experts with specific competence. Numbers do not compensate for sample quality.

options.text: Nothing: scientific consensus is the most reliable form of evidence available
options.explanation: Scientific consensus is robust when it results from convergent independent research. 'Scientists' without specification does not constitute verifiable consensus.

name: Polarization
subtitle: Us vs. Them
summary: Building antagonistic identities that make dialogue impossible.
definition: A strategy of dividing society into two mutually exclusive camps, where group membership matters more than truth.
historicalContext: Used by regimes to consolidate power. In the digital age, polarizing content receives more engagement.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits tribalism: the brain processes information differently depending on whether it comes from 'our group' or the 'opponent'.

variants.name: War terminology
variants.desc: 'Battle', 'front lines' applied to politics

variants.name: Total delegitimization
variants.desc: 'They have no right to speak'

variants.name: Apocalypticism
variants.desc: 'If they win, it's the end'

redFlags: Debate shifted from facts to identity
Systematic 'us' vs 'them'
Internal criticism equated with betrayal

caseStudies.title: The division algorithm
caseStudies.description: Content generating outrage toward the out-group receives more shares. It's a business model.
caseStudies.lesson: Polarization is also an economic model.

defense.action: When you feel part of a 'team', pause
What intermediate positions are being excluded?
It's the first step out of polarization

scenario.title: Speech
scenario.situation: 'Whoever is not with us is against us! There is no middle ground!'
scenario.question: Which category of people does this message make impossible to accommodate?

options.text: Those with nuanced positions, partial criticisms, or doubts — in this schema they do not exist: you are ally or enemy
options.explanation: Polarisation has a practical effect: anyone who does not take sides becomes suspect. This reduces the space for moderate dissent and pushes toward total adherence or abandonment of the field.

options.text: Declared political opponents: it is legitimate to be direct and engage with those who oppose you
options.explanation: Having opponents is normal in political debate. The problem is not the existence of adversaries, but the elimination of the possibility of partial agreement, compromise, or internal criticism.

options.text: Those who have not yet taken a side: neutrality is a form of complicity in certain contexts
options.explanation: This is exactly the kind of argument that polarisation uses to force adherence. Neutrality and doubt are legitimate positions, not complicity.

name: Gish Gallop
subtitle: Flood of Claims
summary: Too many claims too quickly to be verified.
definition: Bombarding the opponent with so many unverified claims that refuting them all is impossible.
historicalContext: Named after creationist Duane Gish, who launched dozens of arguments in a matter of minutes.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits asymmetry: making a claim takes seconds, refuting it takes minutes or hours.

variants.name: Video marathon
variants.desc: Hours of content with hundreds of claims

variants.name: Infinite thread
variants.desc: Dozens of unverified points

variants.name: PDF dump
variants.desc: Links to enormous files: 'It's all in there!'

redFlags: Each point treated in 10-30 seconds
No sources for individual claims
Machine-gun structure

caseStudies.title: Creationist debates
caseStudies.description: Gish would launch dozens of arguments in 5 minutes. Refuting one takes 10 minutes. The audience saw 'unrefuted'.
caseStudies.lesson: The quantity of arguments impresses non-experts.

defense.action: Choose the most important points
'You're making 50 claims in 5 minutes'
If some are false, probably all are

scenario.title: Video
scenario.situation: A 45-minute video lists 80 alleged scandals, one after another, without sources.
scenario.question: Why would responding to all 80 claims be a losing strategy?

options.text: It requires time enormously disproportionate to that needed to make the claims — and whoever responds appears constantly on the defensive, making the accusations seem credible
options.explanation: This is the fundamental asymmetry: making a claim takes seconds, debunking it requires research, sources, and explanation. The attacker has a structural advantage in the dialogue.

options.text: Some might be true — denying everything would be self-contradictory
options.explanation: This is an intended effect: mixing some truths among many falsehoods increases the overall credibility of the pile and makes selective rebuttal more difficult.

options.text: No reason — if the accusations are false it is possible and necessary to refute them all
options.explanation: In theory yes. In practice the economy of attention does not permit it: 80 rebuttals never reach the same audience as the 80 original claims.

name: Straw Man
subtitle: The Scarecrow
summary: Distorting the opponent's argument to attack it more easily.
definition: Building a weak or distorted version of the opponent's argument, attacking that, and pretending to have refuted the original.
historicalContext: The name comes from the ease of knocking down a scarecrow. It is one of the most common fallacies.
psychologicalMechanism: The audience perceives that the argument has been 'refuted', without noticing that the argument attacked was not the original.

variants.name: Oversimplification
variants.desc: Reducing a complex position to a trivial phrase

variants.name: Extremization
variants.desc: Presenting a moderate position as extreme

variants.name: Invention
variants.desc: Attributing to the opponent positions they never held

redFlags: The opponent's position is presented in a ridiculous or extreme way
The attack is easy but doesn't reflect what was actually said
The opponent says 'But I never said that!'

caseStudies.title: Political debates
caseStudies.description: Candidate A proposes minor tax reforms. Candidate B: 'They want to destroy the economy!' Attacks a position A never held.
caseStudies.lesson: Always verify: is the argument being attacked the one actually made?

defense.action: 'Is that what I actually argued?'
'I never said that. My position is...'
Don't defend a position that isn't yours

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: You: 'We should reduce taxes a little.' Opponent: 'You want to eliminate all public services!'
scenario.question: How does the opponent's response change the structure of the debate?

options.text: It transforms a moderate proposal into an extreme position that was never advanced — then attacks that extreme position instead of the original proposal
options.explanation: The advantage is double: the original proposal is not refuted, and the person who made it must now defend a position they never held.

options.text: It highlights the logical implications of the proposal — legitimate criticism even if exaggerated
options.explanation: Logical implications must follow the real causal chain. 'Reduce taxes slightly' does not logically imply 'eliminate all public services' — this is a leap, not an implication.

options.text: It is a legitimate critique: fiscal policies always have effects on public services
options.explanation: True in the abstract — but 'slightly' and 'all' are incompatible quantities. The objection distorts the original proposal instead of analysing its real effects.

name: Red Herring
subtitle: Smoked Herring
summary: Distracting with an irrelevant argument.
definition: Introducing irrelevant information to distract from the main topic.
historicalContext: The name comes from the use of smoked herrings to confuse hunting dogs, drawing them away from the trail.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain follows salient information. By introducing an exciting argument, the main topic is sidelined.

variants.name: Emotional distraction
variants.desc: Shifting to a topic that generates a strong reaction

variants.name: What about
variants.desc: 'And this other problem?'

variants.name: New topic
variants.desc: Completely changing the subject

redFlags: The argument suddenly changes
An unrelated topic is introduced
The response has nothing to do with the question

caseStudies.title: Interviews
caseStudies.description: Journalist: 'What do you say about these scandals?' Politician: 'But look at how wonderful our education reform is!'
caseStudies.lesson: Changing the subject doesn't answer the question.

defense.action: 'That doesn't answer the question'
Repeat the original question
Don't let yourself be drawn onto the new topic

scenario.title: Interview
scenario.situation: Journalist: 'About the corruption investigation?' Politician: 'We should be talking about our economy, which is doing great!'
scenario.question: What does the politician's change of subject reveal about the original question?

options.text: That they have no answer to the question about the investigation, or prefer not to give one — the change of subject is itself informative
options.explanation: In an interview, the respondent controls what comes out. Changing the subject is a choice. The choice to avoid says something about what is being avoided.

options.text: That they consider the economy more urgent than the investigation at this moment
options.explanation: This would be a charitable interpretation — and perhaps it is what the politician wants you to think. But the question was specific and the response does not address it.

options.text: Nothing: politicians have the right to choose what to focus on in an interview
options.explanation: A formal right, yes, but the public also has the right to notice the evasion and draw their own conclusions.

name: Projection
subtitle: Mirror Accusation
summary: Accusing others of exactly what you yourself are doing.
definition: Technique in which one attributes to their opponents exactly what they themselves are doing, thereby neutralizing accusations.
historicalContext: Used systematically by dictatorships and regimes: they accuse the opposition of being 'coup plotters' while preparing a coup.
psychologicalMechanism: Confuses the public ('who is right?') and pre-builds the defense ('they say the same thing'). Makes accusations reciprocal.

variants.name: Mirror accusation
variants.desc: 'You're the one doing X!' while doing it

variants.name: Pre-accusation
variants.desc: Accusing before being accused

variants.name: Inversion
variants.desc: Presenting the victim as the aggressor

redFlags: Accusations that seem perfectly mirrored
The accuser exhibits the same behaviors
Suspicious timing of the accusations

caseStudies.title: Authoritarian regimes
caseStudies.description: Regimes preparing electoral fraud accuse the opposition of 'planning fraud', preemptively delegitimizing accusations that will come.
caseStudies.lesson: Mirror accusations must be verified on both sides.

defense.action: Don't assume 'the truth is in the middle'
Who has evidence, who has only accusations?
Do the accusations arrive before or after?

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: A party accuses another of 'censoring debate'. Simultaneously, it expels internal dissenters.
scenario.question: What strategic advantage does a party gain by accusing its opponent of its own conduct?

options.text: If the accusation becomes a public controversy, both sides seem guilty — actual responsibility is diluted in the confusion
options.explanation: This is the practical function: whoever denounces the original party now appears to be playing partisan politics ('you too though'), and the story is buried in the dispute.

options.text: It serves to balance the debate: the opponent also censors, and that is relevant
options.explanation: If the opponent genuinely censors, it should be documented and denounced — but as a separate, evidenced critique, not a response to the current accusation.

options.text: It reveals that the party is aware of the problem and is trying to address it publicly
options.explanation: On the contrary: it reveals that the party has chosen counter-attack over addressing the specific accusation — suggesting it has no defence for that accusation.

name: Dog Whistle
subtitle: Coded Message
summary: Coded messages that only certain groups understand.
definition: Communications that have an innocent meaning for the general public but a specific message for a target group.
historicalContext: The term comes from dog whistles, audible only to dogs. Used systematically in politics to signal extremist bases.
psychologicalMechanism: Allows controversial messages to be communicated while maintaining plausible deniability ('I didn't mean that').

variants.name: Historical codes
variants.desc: References that only certain groups recognize

variants.name: Charged terms
variants.desc: Words with dual meaning

variants.name: Symbolism
variants.desc: Gestures, numbers, images with specific meaning

redFlags: Messages that seem harmless but generate strong reactions in some groups
Terms with 'secret meanings'
Systematic plausible deniability

caseStudies.title: Election campaigns
caseStudies.description: A candidate uses terms that seem harmless but are recognized by extremist groups as signals of support. If criticized: 'I didn't mean that!'
caseStudies.lesson: Context and the target audience's reaction reveal the real meaning.

defense.action: How do extremist groups react?
Does the term have a dual meaning?
Is the denial plausible?

scenario.title: Speech
scenario.situation: A candidate uses terms that seem normal but are recognized by extremist groups as signals. When criticized: 'But I said a normal phrase!'
scenario.question: Why is deniability a structural part of this technique and not a side effect?

options.text: It allows communication to a specific audience with the benefit of coded understanding, without taking public responsibility for that message
options.explanation: Deniability is designed, not accidental. The message operates on two levels: those who know the code receive the communication; those who do not hear something innocuous.

options.text: The politician used normal words: the criticism shows that those who accuse are finding signals where none exist
options.explanation: This is exactly the intended effect of deniability. But 'normal' and 'coded' are not mutually exclusive: a term can be common in ordinary language and have a specific meaning in an ideological context.

options.text: Coded language exists in all political discourse — not only in extreme forms
options.explanation: True — but not all codes function to activate solidarity with groups that would be politically costly to name explicitly. The distinction matters.

name: Gaslighting
subtitle: Denying Reality
summary: Making victims doubt their own perception of reality.
definition: A manipulation technique that denies obvious facts, minimizes reactions, and makes victims doubt their own judgment.
historicalContext: The term comes from the film 'Gaslight' (1944), where a husband manipulates his wife into thinking she's going crazy.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits dependence on external validation. If constantly denied, one begins to doubt oneself.

variants.name: Denial
variants.desc: 'That never happened!'

variants.name: Minimization
variants.desc: 'You're overreacting!'

variants.name: Inversion
variants.desc: 'You're the one with the problem!'

variants.name: Selective memory
variants.desc: 'I don't remember saying that'

redFlags: Constant denial of obvious facts
Phrases like 'you're too sensitive', 'you imagined everything'
The victim starts doubting their own memory

caseStudies.title: Abusive relationships
caseStudies.description: The abusive partner denies episodes of violence, minimizes the victim's reactions, makes them feel 'crazy'.
caseStudies.lesson: The systematic denial of reality is a form of violence.

defense.action: Record facts and conversations
Seek confirmation from third parties
Systematic denial is a technique, not a misunderstanding

scenario.title: Conversation
scenario.situation: You: 'Yesterday you said X.' Them: 'I never said that! You imagined everything. You're paranoid.' It really happened.
scenario.question: What is the long-term effect of this tactic when used systematically?

options.text: It erodes the victim's trust in their own ability to perceive and remember reality accurately
options.explanation: This is the specific harm: not only is the single dispute won by the manipulator, but the victim begins to distrust their own judgment in the future — making every subsequent resistance harder.

options.text: It creates conflict, but without lasting consequences if the victim knows with certainty what happened
options.explanation: The problem is exactly this: after systematic repetition, certainty wavers. The tactic erodes from within, not from without.

options.text: It resolves misunderstanding: different versions of events are clarified through open dialogue
options.explanation: This interpretation applies to genuine conflict. Here the structure is unilateral: one party deliberately denies what they know to be true. Dialogue does not resolve intentional denial.

name: Cherry-Picking
subtitle: Selective Data
summary: Selecting only favorable data while ignoring everything else.
definition: Selecting evidence that confirms a position while ignoring evidence that refutes it.
historicalContext: The term comes from the idea of picking only the best cherries. Used to delay recognition of risks (tobacco, climate).
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits confirmation bias: we evaluate as more credible information that confirms our existing beliefs.

variants.name: Truncated time series
variants.desc: Showing only a favorable period

variants.name: Outliers as the norm
variants.desc: Exceptional case presented as representative

variants.name: Quotes out of context
variants.desc: Phrase that changes meaning in context

redFlags: Charts without complete time axes
A statistic cited without context
Absence of dissenting data

caseStudies.title: Tobacco
caseStudies.description: The industry cited studies that showed uncertainty, ignoring the growing scientific consensus.
caseStudies.lesson: Cherry-picking can create the illusion of debate.

defense.action: What is the full historical series?
Find the original study
What data is NOT being shown?

scenario.title: Press release
scenario.situation: 'Historic record! +150,000 jobs!' Doesn't mention that 200,000 people lost jobs in the same period.
scenario.question: Is the press release lying?

options.text: No — the cited figure is verifiable and real, but the omission of context produces an impression opposite to overall reality
options.explanation: This distinction is fundamental for fact-checking: direct rebuttal does not work because the figure is true. You must add context, not contradict the figure.

options.text: Yes — presenting a figure without relevant context is equivalent to an intentional falsehood
options.explanation: Ethically it may be. But legally and practically the difference matters: rebuttals work on falsehoods, not on omissions. Debunking this requires completing the story, not denying it.

options.text: It cannot be determined without the full press release
options.explanation: We already know that '150,000 jobs created out of 200,000 lost' tells the opposite story from '150,000 jobs created' in isolation. The critical omission is already identifiable.

name: Astroturfing
subtitle: Fake Grassroots
summary: Simulating a grassroots movement that is actually coordinated from the top.
definition: Creating movements, campaigns, or opinions that appear spontaneous but are orchestrated.
historicalContext: Term from the 1980s when companies created groups of 'concerned citizens' against environmental regulations.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits social proof: we tend to conform to what we perceive as majority opinion.

variants.name: Bot networks
variants.desc: Coordinated automated accounts

variants.name: Troll farms
variants.desc: Centers where people manage dozens of accounts

variants.name: Review bombing
variants.desc: Wave of fake reviews

redFlags: Recently created accounts with sudden high activity
Nearly identical language across different profiles
Coordinated timing

caseStudies.title: IRA
caseStudies.description: The Internet Research Agency managed hundreds of accounts posing as American activists, amplifying divisions.
caseStudies.lesson: Astroturfing can aim only to undermine trust.

defense.action: Creation date, number of posts, consistency
Similar language, coordinated timing
Don't be impressed by numbers

scenario.title: Coordinated comments
scenario.situation: Under a critical post, 100 profiles with identical phrases. Many have 3-10 total posts, created in the same month.
scenario.question: Why is the coordination of the profiles more relevant than the content of the individual comments?

options.text: It simulates an organic consensus that does not exist: it falsifies the perception of public opinion, not just a single comment
options.explanation: In a democratic system, the weight of opinion comes from its independence, not its quantity. A hundred identical voices are worth less than ten diverse ones.

options.text: The content is wrong: that is what matters, not how many people repeat it
options.explanation: This is true for analysing the merits — but the operation is not trying to persuade through reasoning; it is trying to create the impression of widespread grassroots consensus.

options.text: New profiles do not imply falsehood: anyone can create an account
options.explanation: True for a single profile. But the combination of new accounts + identical messages + coordinated timing has a very low statistical probability of being organic.

name: False Flag
subtitle: Covert Operations
summary: Actions presented as coming from another party.
definition: Operations conducted to appear as if carried out by another actor.
historicalContext: Naval term: sailing under a false flag. Historical examples: Reichstag, Gleiwitz.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the need to respond to threats: if we perceive an attack from X, we support actions against X.

variants.name: Clone sites
variants.desc: Replicas of sites with false content

variants.name: Infiltrated accounts
variants.desc: Profiles faking membership

variants.name: Provocations
variants.desc: Orchestrated protests to delegitimize

redFlags: Event that justifies already-planned actions
Immediate attribution without investigation
Document that seems 'too perfect'

caseStudies.title: Operation Northwoods
caseStudies.description: In 1962, US military chiefs proposed false flag operations against American civilians to be blamed on Cuba. Rejected by Kennedy.
caseStudies.lesson: False flags are documented historical plans.

defense.action: Is it the official site?
Don't accept immediate attributions
Who gains? Is it 'too convenient'?

scenario.title: Leaked document
scenario.situation: A PDF circulates: internal memorandum with cynical strategies. Anonymous account, no verifiable letterhead, one week before elections.
scenario.question: Which element of this scenario is most significant for assessing the document's authenticity?

options.text: The timing relative to the election — it increases the motive for fabrication right now, regardless of the content
options.explanation: Timing is the signal hardest to fake casually. An authentic document can surface at any time. One constructed for maximum impact will appear close to a deadline.

options.text: The bluntness of the described strategies — authentic internal documents often contain this kind of language
options.explanation: True — but so do forgeries. The verisimilitude of the content is exactly what a forger maximises.

options.text: The online spread — a false document would not manage to circulate so widely
options.explanation: On the contrary: forgeries designed for maximum spread often circulate more than authentic documents, precisely because they are built to make news.

name: Deepfake
subtitle: Video Manipulation
summary: Creating AI-generated content indistinguishable from reality.
definition: Content produced with AI that replaces faces, voices, or movements.
historicalContext: Technology accessible since 2017. Initially for pornographic content, then politics.
psychologicalMechanism: 'Seeing is believing': images have higher perceptual authority than text.

variants.name: Face swap
variants.desc: Face replacement

variants.name: Lip sync
variants.desc: Lip synchronization

variants.name: Voice cloning
variants.desc: Voice replication

redFlags: Unnatural facial movements
Imperfect synchronization
Graphical artifacts

caseStudies.title: Zelensky
caseStudies.description: A deepfake video showed Zelensky ordering surrender. Imperfect quality but required an official denial.
caseStudies.lesson: Political deepfakes are reality, not future speculation.

defense.action: Where did it come from?
Are eyes, hands, edges flawed?
For sensational videos, wait for confirmation

scenario.title: Sensational video
scenario.situation: A video shows a leader insulting voters. Source: anonymous account. Good but not perfect quality.
scenario.question: Is adding a disclaimer and resharing a responsible response?

options.text: No — it amplifies the spread and disclaimers are often ignored or lost in subsequent reshares
options.explanation: Research on misinformation shows that disclaimers do not neutralise the effect of false content once seen. Resharing 'to debunk' is one of the main spreading mechanisms.

options.text: Yes — it is responsible to inform the public without censoring, leaving evaluation to each person
options.explanation: This logic applies to ambiguous content. For suspected deepfakes, every reshare increases exposure and therefore potential harm.

options.text: It depends on video quality: if it is high, the video is probably authentic
options.explanation: Deepfake quality is constantly improving. In 2024, accessible tools produce results that are hard to distinguish. Quality is not a criterion of authenticity.

name: Influence Operations
subtitle: Information Warfare
summary: Coordinated campaigns to influence opinions and elections.
definition: Coordinated campaigns by states or actors to shape public opinion and electoral outcomes.
historicalContext: Interference has existed since the Cold War. The digital novelty is the scale and direct action on individual citizens.
psychologicalMechanism: Amplify pre-existing divisions rather than creating new ones.

variants.name: Division amplification
variants.desc: Promoting polarizing content

variants.name: Voter suppression
variants.desc: Demotivating segments from going to vote

variants.name: Hack and leak
variants.desc: Stealing and selectively publishing

redFlags: Sites that imitate existing media
Identical narratives in multiple languages simultaneously
Sources with large resources and zero transparency

caseStudies.title: Clone networks
caseStudies.description: Operations have created dozens of sites imitating European media with fake articles.
caseStudies.lesson: Always check the exact domain.

defense.action: Is it the official site?
Has the site existed for long?
Content perfectly aligned with your opinions deserves extra skepticism

scenario.title: Suspicious site
scenario.situation: 'TheNews-Daily.com' publishes an explosive investigation. Domain registered two months ago, 'about us' page with no real names.
scenario.question: The investigation seems well-documented and specific. Is that sufficient to share it?

options.text: No — the credibility of the content and the credibility of the source are separate questions; both require verification
options.explanation: Sophisticated influence operations produce accurate, well-documented content — precisely to evade first-level fact-checking. The source matters as much as the reported facts.

options.text: Yes — the quality of the content is the best evidence of journalistic seriousness
options.explanation: This is the trap: a sophisticated operation builds editorial credibility in order to abuse it at critical moments. Average quality does not guarantee integrity on any specific piece.

options.text: Only if other media pick it up — that would certify its authenticity
options.explanation: Resharing increases the perception of credibility, not the authenticity. If the original source is fabricated, multiplication of reshares does not make it true.

name: Flooding
subtitle: Digital Flooding
summary: Drowning debate with noise and toxicity.
definition: Flooding comments, posts, and discussions with content that stifles debate.
historicalContext: A central technique of modern 'troll farms'.
psychologicalMechanism: The noise exhausts those seeking serious information, who eventually disengage.

variants.name: Spam
variants.desc: Thousands of identical messages

variants.name: Coordinated trolling
variants.desc: Systematic insults and ridicule

variants.name: Hashtag hijacking
variants.desc: Hijacking conversations with hashtags

redFlags: Disproportionate volume of negative comments
Accounts appearing only on certain topics
Coordinated timing

caseStudies.title: Organized comments
caseStudies.description: In some countries, armies of commenters systematically attack critical voices.
caseStudies.lesson: Volume does not reflect consensus.

defense.action: Don't feed the flooding
Filters, blocks, reports
Moderated platforms or threads

scenario.title: Invaded discussion
scenario.situation: A serious discussion is flooded with 500 insulting comments within hours. New accounts, similar messages.
scenario.question: Do 500 opposing comments indicate that the criticised position is wrong?

options.text: No — coordinated quantity substitutes diversity: it simulates a majority without representing real, independent opinions
options.explanation: In a democratic system, the weight of opinion derives from its independence, not its quantity. A hundred identical voices are worth less than ten diverse ones.

options.text: Yes — in democracy the majority counts, even in online discussions
options.explanation: Democracy counts the votes of real people with independently formed opinions. Coordinated profiles are not 'real people' in this sense — they are an operation.

options.text: It depends: if the comments are relevant to the topic, they represent real opinions
options.explanation: Relevance to the topic is a necessary but not sufficient condition. Coordinated operations also write topically relevant comments. The relevant signal is the coordination, not the content.

name: Fear Appeal
subtitle: Appeal to Fear
summary: Mobilizing through fear instead of argument.
definition: Technique that uses fear to induce actions without critical thinking.
historicalContext: Foundation of advertising ('buy this or something bad happens') and politics ('if not X, disaster').
psychologicalMechanism: Fear activates the sympathetic nervous system, bypassing reflective thought.

variants.name: Existential fear
variants.desc: 'If not X, it's the end'

variants.name: Personal fear
variants.desc: 'You or your loved ones...'

variants.name: Apocalypticism
variants.desc: Every alternative is catastrophe

redFlags: Messages that generate strong anxiety
Extreme urgency
No analysis of alternatives

caseStudies.title: Fear advertising
caseStudies.description: Campaigns showing terrible consequences if you don't buy the product/service.
caseStudies.lesson: Fear sells, but doesn't inform.

defense.action: Am I feeling anxious? That is intentional.
Decisions based on fear are often wrong
Reality is more nuanced than the apocalypse

scenario.title: Message
scenario.situation: 'SHARE NOW or your daughter could be in danger! You have only a few hours!'
