scenario.question: Which cognitive mechanism does this message exploit to obtain immediate sharing without verification?

options.text: Fear for the safety of loved ones, which blocks critical evaluation and drives impulsive action
options.explanation: Fear is one of the emotions most effective at bypassing deliberate reasoning. Messages built on threats to children exploit precisely this mechanism.

options.text: It provides urgent information: when there is a real danger, speed matters more than verification
options.explanation: This is the idea the message wants to create. But a genuinely important public safety warning reaches through official channels, not through viral messages without a source.

options.text: It activates social responsibility: those who do not share bear the guilt for any harm
options.explanation: This is a deliberate secondary mechanism: the anticipated guilt for not sharing is a second pressure layer. But it is built on fear, not an independent mechanism.

name: Flag-Waving
subtitle: Appeal to Nationalism
summary: Exploiting patriotism to legitimize causes.
definition: Associating a cause with the flag, nation, or national symbols to make it beyond question.
historicalContext: A classic technique: those who oppose 'don't love their country'.
psychologicalMechanism: National identity runs deep. Attacking it or opposing something wrapped in the flag feels almost like betrayal.

variants.name: National symbols
variants.desc: Flags, anthem, national colors

variants.name: Patriotism vs. betrayal
variants.desc: Those who disagree are 'unpatriotic'

variants.name: History and glory
variants.desc: Invoking national greatness

redFlags: Arguments wrapped in the flag
Criticism = anti-patriotism
Excessive use of national symbols

caseStudies.title: Iraq War
caseStudies.description: Criticism of the war presented as 'not supporting the troops' or 'not loving America'.
caseStudies.lesson: You can love your country and criticize its actions.

defense.action: The cause and the country are different things
Is it an argument or an emotional appeal?
Criticism ≠ betrayal

scenario.title: Debate
scenario.situation: Someone criticizes a government policy. The opponent: 'If you don't like this country, go somewhere else!'
scenario.question: What does this response do to the original political criticism?

options.text: Ignores it completely — shifts the debate from evaluation of a specific policy to the critic's national loyalty
options.explanation: The move is effective because it puts the critic on the defensive on different terrain: they must now defend their patriotism instead of continuing the criticism.

options.text: Responds indirectly: those who criticise the government have a public responsibility that should be noted
options.explanation: Public responsibility in criticism is verified through content, not national identity. The response shifts the plane of discourse to avoid the content.

options.text: It is a valid argument: loyalty to institutions is a prerequisite for legitimate criticism in a democracy
options.explanation: On the contrary: in democracies, criticism of institutions is one of the most important forms of civic participation. Conditioning it on loyalty is a form of silencing.

name: Beautiful People
subtitle: Successful People
summary: Associating beauty and success with a cause.
definition: Showing attractive, happy, successful people associated with a product, candidate, or idea.
historicalContext: The foundation of modern advertising. Extended to politics.
psychologicalMechanism: Emotional transfer: if those people are happy and support X, supporting X will make me happy too.

variants.name: Beauty
variants.desc: Attractive people associated with the cause

variants.name: Success
variants.desc: Wealthy/famous people who endorse

variants.name: Happiness
variants.desc: Happy people who use/vote

redFlags: Images of attractive people without connection to quality
Associating happiness with products/policies
Celebrities endorsing without competence

caseStudies.title: Political advertising
caseStudies.description: Candidates surrounded by young, beautiful, happy people in their campaign spots.
caseStudies.lesson: The image says nothing about the proposals.

defense.action: What is being proposed, not who is proposing it
Is the person's beauty relevant?
Behind the image, what are the actual proposals?

scenario.title: Campaign
scenario.situation: A spot shows beautiful, happy people dancing with the party logo. No policy is discussed.
scenario.question: What should a critical viewer ask about this ad that the ad does not answer?

options.text: What are the party's concrete proposals — the ad does not answer because it does not want you to ask before forming a positive impression
options.explanation: The ad builds an emotional association (beauty, happiness, music, logo) designed to precede rational evaluation. The question 'but what do they propose?' has no answer in the message.

options.text: Whether the people are paid actors or real supporters
options.explanation: A legitimate question, but not the most critical. Even with real supporters, the fundamental problem remains: no proposal is communicated.

options.text: Why the party doesn't use its own leaders instead of anonymous people
options.explanation: Leaders might evoke negative reactions. The choice to use neutral faces is deliberate — but the fundamental problem remains the absence of content, not the absence of leaders.

name: Scarcity Principle
subtitle: Artificial Urgency
summary: Creating artificial urgency to prevent critical reflection and force impulsive decisions.
definition: The scarcity principle exploits loss aversion: the brain assigns more value to things that are hard to get or running out. Manipulators artificially create this condition to short-circuit rational thinking.
historicalContext: Theorized by Robert Cialdini in 1984 as one of the six principles of persuasion. Used by salespeople, fraudsters, and in interrogation contexts to accelerate decisions against the victim's interest.
psychologicalMechanism: Activates loss aversion bias: losing something weighs psychologically about twice as much as an equivalent gain. Urgency bypasses the prefrontal cortex (slow, rational thinking) and activates rapid emotional responses.

variants.name: Time scarcity
variants.desc: 'Offer expires in one hour', artificial countdown timers

variants.name: Quantity scarcity
variants.desc: 'Only 2 spots left', 'Last items available'

variants.name: Exclusivity
variants.desc: 'Only for our premium customers', fictitious limited access

variants.name: Information scarcity
variants.desc: 'Share before they delete it', 'secret' documents

redFlags: Presence of countdowns or sudden deadlines
Pressure to decide 'now' with no time to reflect
An offer that won't be available if you don't act immediately
Racing heart: you are using your fast brain

caseStudies.title: Interrogations and negotiations
caseStudies.description: 'This is your only chance to cooperate' creates false scarcity of opportunity, inducing rushed decisions under stress.
caseStudies.lesson: Genuine offers survive time. If you can't wait, it's a trap.

defense.action: Is your heart racing? That's a signal: someone is activating your System 1
'I need time to think about it' — a real offer will still be valid tomorrow
Is the urgency verifiable or simply asserted?

scenario.title: Commercial negotiation
scenario.situation: 'This price is only valid today. My manager gave me authorization only for this morning. Then it goes back to full price.'
scenario.question: What is the most reliable signal that this deadline is not real?

options.text: A genuinely limited offer can wait for the time needed to think — the pressure to decide 'now' serves to prevent reflection, not to inform you of a real limit
options.explanation: Real deadlines exist — but they are usually communicated in advance and can be verified. A deadline constructed on the spot is designed to activate impulse, not to inform.

options.text: The salesperson cites their manager: this adds credibility to the deadline
options.explanation: On the contrary: the hierarchical chain ('my manager said') is a standard technique for making the deadline external and non-negotiable. It is rarely verifiable on the spot.

options.text: The price: if it were truly competitive, urgency wouldn't be necessary
options.explanation: Not necessarily true — genuine limited offers exist. The price is not the right indicator; the structure of the time pressure is.

name: Reciprocity Principle
subtitle: The Emotional Debt
summary: Exploiting the sense of social obligation: an unsolicited favor creates the psychological need to reciprocate.
definition: The principle of reciprocity is a universal social norm: if someone does us a favor, we feel indebted and want to give something back. Manipulators use small gifts or strategic favors to create this sense of obligation and then ask for something much larger in return.
historicalContext: Documented by Robert Cialdini and cultural anthropology as a universal norm. Systematically exploited in informant recruitment, cults, and aggressive sales techniques.
psychologicalMechanism: The obligation to reciprocate is deeply rooted evolutionarily: communities that cooperated survived better. The guilt for not reciprocating is a mechanism of social coercion that the manipulator turns into leverage.

variants.name: Unexpected gift
variants.desc: Unsolicited gift before an important request

variants.name: Strategic favor
variants.desc: Practical help that creates emotional dependency

variants.name: Apparent concession
variants.desc: Reduction of the initial request to seem reasonable

variants.name: Compliment-request
variants.desc: Sudden praise immediately before asking an uncomfortable favor

redFlags: Gift or unsolicited favor immediately followed by a request
Guilt if you don't 'reciprocate' something you didn't ask for
Suspicious timing: favor and request close together
The request is much larger than the favor received

caseStudies.title: Informant recruitment
caseStudies.description: Intelligence services use reciprocity systematically: first small favors (a visa, useful information), then growing requests for classified information.
caseStudies.lesson: You have the right to accept a favor without feeling obligated to give something back. Reciprocity is a social norm, not a contract.

defense.action: Did the favor arrive just before a request? It might not be coincidental.
'I received X, but I have no obligation to give Y' — the debt is psychological, not real.
You can accept kindness without buying anything in return.

scenario.title: Colleague at work
scenario.situation: A colleague who has never helped you before brings you coffee, compliments your work, then asks you to cover for an unauthorized absence.
scenario.question: What should you remember when you feel guilty for not reciprocating an unsolicited favour?

options.text: An unsolicited favour creates no real obligation: the debt you feel is a psychological construct, not a contract
options.explanation: The norm of reciprocity is powerful and universal — but it applies to consensual exchanges. A strategic favour is not a gift: it is an investment. You can accept the investment without paying the expected return.

options.text: That you have the right to reciprocate, but you can propose something different from what is being asked
options.explanation: This approach accepts the premise of the debt. The point is that there are no debts to settle — only the psychological pressure of a social norm being strategically exploited.

options.text: That the working relationship might deteriorate if you do not accommodate your colleague
options.explanation: This concern may be real in certain contexts, but accepting it as a reason means being manipulated by the second layer of the technique: the implicit threat to relationships.

name: Anchoring Effect
subtitle: The Rigged Starting Point
summary: The first piece of information received disproportionately conditions all subsequent evaluations.
definition: The anchoring effect describes the brain's tendency to rely excessively on the first available information. Once the anchor is set, all subsequent evaluations are measured relative to it, even if the anchor is arbitrary or false.
historicalContext: Demonstrated by Kahneman and Tversky in 1974 as one of the main cognitive biases. Used systematically in negotiations, interrogations, advertising, and legal proceedings.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain uses the first piece of information as a reference point to save cognitive energy. Subsequent adjustment away from the anchor is almost always insufficient: even knowing about the anchoring effect, we cannot fully correct for it.

variants.name: Price anchor
variants.desc: Inflated initial price to make the real price seem 'affordable'

variants.name: Extreme anchor
variants.desc: Absurd request to make the real one seem reasonable

variants.name: Numerical anchor
variants.desc: Any number in the conversation influences subsequent estimates

variants.name: Interrogation anchor
variants.desc: Very serious initial accusations to obtain 'minor' admissions

redFlags: The conversation starts with an extreme number or request
You feel relieved when a 'reduced' proposal arrives
You're evaluating how much you saved, not whether the price is fair
The first offer was so unreasonable that anything else seems reasonable

caseStudies.title: Hostage negotiation
caseStudies.description: Negotiators know that the first figure communicated becomes the anchor. Whoever makes the first offer controls the gravitational field of the negotiation.
caseStudies.lesson: Ignore the first offer and evaluate the current one for what it is, not for the savings relative to the anchor.

defense.action: If the first figure or request was absurd, mentally delete it before evaluating the new one.
'If I hadn't heard the first proposal, would this one seem fair?'
In a negotiation, make the first offer yourself to control the reference point.

scenario.title: Car purchase
scenario.situation: The dealer first shows a €45,000 car, then offers you the one you want at €28,000. It seems like a great deal.
scenario.question: If you had not seen the first car at €45,000, how would you evaluate the €28,000 price?

options.text: Based on the real market value — not on the saving relative to a figure constructed by the dealer to become your reference point
options.explanation: This is the mental 'reset' that the anchoring effect makes difficult. The problem is not whether €28,000 is much or little in absolute terms — it is that it is being evaluated relative to an arbitrary anchor, not to the market.

options.text: It would not change: a low price is a bargain regardless of context
options.explanation: This presupposes that €28,000 is 'low' — but low compared to what? The €45,000 anchor or the real value? Without a market comparison, we do not know.

options.text: It would be harder to evaluate without a reference point
options.explanation: The correct reference point is not the other car in the showroom — it is the market price for the same model. Having fewer anchors means having to find the right reference, not being left without information.

name: Confirmation Bias
subtitle: The Cognitive Bubble
summary: Seeking and overvaluing information that confirms what we already believe, ignoring information that contradicts us.
definition: Confirmation bias is the tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information in ways that confirm pre-existing beliefs. Manipulators don't need to lie: they just filter reality to feed only what reinforces already-held convictions.
historicalContext: Documented by Peter Wason in 1960 and developed by Kahneman. It is the cognitive foundation on which contemporary propaganda is built: social media algorithms amplify it automatically, creating echo chambers.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain uses pre-existing beliefs as a perceptual filter. Consistent information is processed more easily (cognitive fluency), contradictory information generates discomfort (cognitive dissonance) and is discarded. We feel 'smart' while actually following a laid-out path.

variants.name: Echo chamber
variants.desc: Information environment where only similar views circulate

variants.name: Algorithmic filtering
variants.desc: Algorithms that amplify already-liked content

variants.name: Selective confirmation
variants.desc: Actively seeking only sources that confirm one's position

variants.name: Biased reading
variants.desc: Interpreting ambiguous information as confirmation of one's thesis

redFlags: All your sources think exactly like you
You feel 'finally informed' compared to those who 'don't get it'
The counter-argument seems absurd without having analyzed it
The news you receive always proves you right

caseStudies.title: Propaganda and information control
caseStudies.description: Authoritarian regimes don't only censor: they selectively feed the confirmation biases of internal opponents to make them predictable and controllable. Those who believe they are 'awake' are following exactly the path laid out for them.
caseStudies.lesson: If everything you read proves you right, you're probably living in a manipulated bubble.

defense.action: Actively find the best argument against your position
If all your sources agree, add dissenting voices
'Who benefits from me believing this information?'

scenario.title: Social media
scenario.situation: Every day your feed shows articles that confirm your political views. You feel increasingly certain you are right and increasingly convinced that those who think differently are misinformed.
scenario.question: After months of this feed, your growing certainty about your political positions — is that a positive sign or a warning?

options.text: A warning: those who inform themselves on complex topics using diverse sources usually recognise more nuance — not more certainty
options.explanation: Growing certainty on structurally complex topics like politics is statistically suspicious. It indicates selective exposure to confirming information, not learning that adds complexity.

options.text: Positive: certainty based on consistent information means you have developed a documented worldview
options.explanation: Consistency among sources does not indicate accuracy if those sources were selected by the algorithm because they already agree with you. Consistency is an effect of the filter, not of quality.

options.text: Neutral: it depends on the accuracy of the sources you are following
options.explanation: This answer presupposes you can evaluate the accuracy of sources from inside the bubble — but that is precisely what the bubble makes difficult.

name: False Dilemma
subtitle: The Black-and-White Trap
summary: Artificially reducing a complex situation to just two extreme opposites, eliminating all intermediate alternatives.
definition: The false dilemma (black-and-white fallacy) is a logical fallacy that presents a situation as if only two options exist — typically one acceptable and one unacceptable — deliberately eliminating the space for nuance, intermediate positions, and alternative solutions.
historicalContext: A central technique in war rhetoric and totalitarianism: 'Either you're with us or you're against us' (Bush, 2001). Used systematically to eliminate moderate positions and force people to pick a side.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the brain's evolutionary tendency toward binary categorization (safe/dangerous). Under stress or perceived urgency, thinking simplifies. Eliminating alternatives creates a false sense that a choice is necessary.

variants.name: Either with us or against us
variants.desc: Eliminates any neutral or critical position

variants.name: Asymmetric either-or
variants.desc: One of the two options is clearly worse than the other

variants.name: False choice
variants.desc: Options presented as exhaustive but they're not

variants.name: Forced moral dilemma
variants.desc: Complex situation reduced to absolute good vs. absolute evil

redFlags: Repeated use of 'either... or...' with no third option
Those who don't choose are labeled traitors or cowards
Moderate positions presented as impossible or dishonest
Constructed urgency to prevent reflection on alternatives

caseStudies.title: Post-9/11 rhetoric
caseStudies.description: The formula 'either you're with us or you're with the terrorists' eliminated any space for reasoned criticism of anti-terrorism policies, equating dissent with complicity.
caseStudies.lesson: Complex situations rarely have only two outcomes. The third option — often the most articulate — is intentionally eliminated.

defense.action: Explicitly identify the omitted alternatives: 'Options C, D, and E also exist'
'Why would these be the only two possible options?'
Don't accept the playing field: 'This isn't a binary choice'

scenario.title: Security debate
scenario.situation: A politician: 'Either you support this security law, or you want criminals to roam free. There's no middle ground.'
scenario.question: What does presenting only two options reveal about the real nature of the problem?

options.text: That the speaker has an interest in preventing discussion of alternatives that might not serve their goal
options.explanation: Binary dilemmas are extremely rare in public policy. Presenting one almost always means that the 'either A or B' framing is constructed to exclude C, D, E — solutions that might be more effective but less useful to whoever is applying pressure.

options.text: That the issue is genuinely binary: on security there are no shades of grey
options.explanation: This is exactly the premise the dilemma wants you to accept. In reality, security policies always involve trade-offs, gradations, and alternatives — they are among the most debated topics in democracies.

options.text: That the politician is experienced: simplifying problems is a governance quality
options.explanation: Simplification that aids understanding is different from simplification that eliminates real options. Presenting only two choices on a complex topic is not effective communication: it is a reduction of decision space.

name: Love Bombing
subtitle: The Love Bomb
summary: Overwhelming someone with intense attention, compliments, and affection to create emotional dependency and lower critical defenses.
definition: Love bombing is a manipulation technique in which someone floods a target with excessive, deliberate, and strategic attention, praise, affection, and total availability. The goal is to create emotional dependency, accelerate the bond, and lower critical defenses before beginning to control or exploit the person.
historicalContext: Initially documented in religious cult dynamics of the 1970s (Moon, Scientology). Now recognized as the initial phase of narcissistic and abusive relationships, as well as a recruitment technique in extremist organizations.
psychologicalMechanism: Activates dopamine circuits with unpredictable and intense rewards. The brain associates the source of all that affection with security and personal value. When the bombing ceases — as it always does — the created dependency pushes the person to do anything to recover it.

variants.name: Romantic
variants.desc: Exaggerated intensity in romantic relationships: constant messages, early declarations, 'you're unique'

variants.name: Sectarian
variants.desc: The community welcomes you with total warmth to isolate you from the outside world

variants.name: Professional
variants.desc: Superior who praises excessively to create absolute loyalty

variants.name: Digital
variants.desc: Influencers or communities that overwhelm you with validation to create platform dependency

redFlags: Emotional intensity disproportionate to the duration of the relationship
Declarations of love or loyalty extraordinarily early
Attempts to monopolize your time and isolate you from other relationships
Feeling of being 'finally understood' after very little time
Withdrawal of attention used as punishment

caseStudies.title: Cult recruitment
caseStudies.description: 'Love bombing' is the first phase of the thought control cycle documented by Robert Lifton: warm welcome, total sense of community, responses to all your emotional needs — before the demands begin.
caseStudies.lesson: Unusual emotional intensity is a warning sign, not confirmation of uniqueness. Healthy relationships are built gradually.

defense.action: Is the emotional intensity proportionate to how long you've known each other?
Try saying no to something: does the affection remain or turn into pressure?
Be wary of relationships that want to monopolize your time and isolate you

scenario.title: New relationship
scenario.situation: After two weeks, someone texts you every hour, calls you 'the love of my life', organizes every one of your weekends, and says the people in your previous life 'didn't understand you the way they do'.
scenario.question: Why is emotional intensity in the first weeks a warning sign rather than a sign of compatibility?

options.text: It creates emotional dependency before a realistic judgment of the other person forms — and progressive isolation reinforces this dependency
options.explanation: A healthy relationship is built on progressive mutual knowledge. Extreme early intensity skips this phase: it creates a strong bond on non-existent foundations, then uses it as leverage.

options.text: It means the person is particularly empathetic and attentive to others' needs
options.explanation: Genuine empathy respects the other person's pace. Unilateral intensity and the speed with which 'love of my life' is declared are signs of projection, not of knowing someone.

options.text: It depends: some people express love more intensely than others by nature
options.explanation: True — but here it is not only the intensity. It is intensity combined with systematic isolation from existing support networks. This specific combination is the pattern to recognise.

name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
subtitle: The Sunk Cost Trap
summary: Continuing a wrong course of action because 'we've already invested too much', ignoring future costs and better alternatives.
definition: The sunk cost fallacy is the cognitive bias by which we continue to invest time, money, or energy in something that isn't working, solely because we have already invested a great deal. Past unrecoverable costs are treated as a reason not to change course, even when it is rationally better to do so.
historicalContext: Documented by Kahneman and Tversky in prospect theory. Systematically exploited to sustain wars ('we can't withdraw, too many soldiers have died'), in casinos, in abusive relationships, and in installment sales.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain registers losses with roughly twice the intensity of equivalent gains (loss aversion). Abandoning an investment feels like 'admitting a mistake' — psychological pain we avoid by continuing to invest, turning a loss into a total waste.

variants.name: Financial
variants.desc: Holding losing stocks: 'I've already lost too much to sell now'

variants.name: Relational
variants.desc: Staying in toxic relationships: 'I've invested 10 years in this person'

variants.name: Military/political
variants.desc: Continuing failed wars or policies: 'We can't withdraw — they would have died in vain'

variants.name: Project-based
variants.desc: Continuing failed projects: 'We've already spent too much to stop now'

redFlags: Justification based on 'I've already spent/invested too much'
Future decisions conditioned by unrecoverable past losses
Difficulty changing course even when it's rationally better to do so
Confusing 'admitting the mistake' with 'wasting the investment'

caseStudies.title: Concorde
caseStudies.description: The British and French governments continued funding Concorde even after realizing it would never be commercially viable, because 'too much had already been invested'. The total cost was enormously greater than what stopping would have cost.
caseStudies.lesson: Past costs are unrecoverable: the correct decision always looks only at future costs and benefits.

defense.action: 'If I were starting from scratch today, would I make this choice?' — the past doesn't count for future decisions
Evaluate only future costs and benefits, not those already incurred
Changing course doesn't mean wasting: it means stopping multiplying the error

scenario.title: Corporate project
scenario.situation: A project has already consumed €200,000 and 18 months of work. Analyses show it will never be profitable. The CEO says: 'We can't abandon it now, we've already invested too much.'
scenario.question: Why should the €200,000 already spent not influence this decision?

options.text: Because those funds are unrecoverable: the correct decision is based only on future costs and benefits, not on what has already been lost
options.explanation: This is the economic principle of sunk costs. Continuing an unprofitable project to 'avoid waste' of what is already spent means wasting future costs too.

options.text: Because there is no definitive proof yet that the project will fail — negative projections are often wrong
options.explanation: Uncertainty about projections is real, but it is a separate argument. If there is sufficient evidence of non-profitability, past investment does not make it more promising.

options.text: Because responsibility to the employees involved matters more than profit
options.explanation: This too is a separate and legitimate argument — but it is different from the sunk cost reasoning. Conflating the two is an additional cognitive error.

name: Halo Effect
subtitle: The Blinding Glow
summary: Attributing positive qualities in every domain to a person or product based on a single positive characteristic.
definition: The halo effect is a cognitive bias by which a single positive (or negative) characteristic of a person or entity disproportionately influences the overall perception of them. Physically attractive people are perceived as more intelligent, honest, and competent. Those successful in one field are considered experts in all others.
historicalContext: Discovered by Edward Thorndike in 1920 in military studies: officers rated attractive soldiers as more intelligent and trustworthy. Today it is the foundation of aspirational marketing, the influencer economy, and cults of personality.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain uses cognitive shortcuts to evaluate quickly: if one characteristic is positive, it extends the positive evaluation to other unverified attributes (overgeneralization). The opposite effect — the horn effect — works symmetrically with negative characteristics.

variants.name: Beauty-competence
variants.desc: Attractive people perceived as more intelligent and trustworthy

variants.name: Success-wisdom
variants.desc: Billionaires or celebrities considered experts in everything

variants.name: National-quality
variants.desc: Products from certain countries perceived as superior in every field

variants.name: Horn effect (inverse)
variants.desc: A negative characteristic contaminates the overall perception

redFlags: Trusting someone's opinions on topics far from their competence
Evaluating the quality of an argument based on the speaker's appearance or success
Believing anything someone says because 'they did X well'
Admiring or distrusting an entire brand based on a single product

caseStudies.title: Celebrity endorsement
caseStudies.description: Companies pay huge sums to have an actor or athlete associated with their product, even if the person has no expertise in the sector. The halo of success transfers to the brand.
caseStudies.lesson: Success in one field does not guarantee competence in another. Always separate who says something from what they say.

defense.action: 'What exactly are they expert in? Does this topic fall within their verified competence?'
Focus on the content of what is being said, not on who is saying it
Success in X does not transfer competence to Y

scenario.title: Investment advice
scenario.situation: A famous actor promotes a cryptocurrency investment on social media. 'If they've succeeded in life, they know what they're doing.'
scenario.question: Why is the reasoning 'he has succeeded in life, so he knows what he's doing' fallacious?

options.text: Because success in one domain does not transfer competence to a completely different one — excelling at acting does not imply expertise in financial analysis
options.explanation: The brain generalises credibility ('good at everything') — this is the heuristic being exploited. But competence is specific: a great surgeon is not necessarily a good economist, and vice versa.

options.text: Because it involves risky investments and an actor's advice should be taken with caution
options.explanation: Partially correct, but it does not identify the mechanism. The problem is not just the risk level: notoriety is not a credential for any type of investment, risky or otherwise.

options.text: Because actors are paid to promote products and are therefore not credible
options.explanation: This would be true if there were a declared sponsorship. The problem here is the follower's reasoning ('he succeeded'), not necessarily the actor's conduct.

name: Appeal to Nature
subtitle: The Natural Fallacy
summary: Assuming that what is 'natural' is automatically good, healthy, or right, and that what is 'artificial' is bad or dangerous.
definition: The appeal to nature is a logical fallacy that asserts or implies that something is good, right, or superior because it is 'natural', and bad or inferior because it is 'artificial' or 'synthetic'. It doesn't consider that naturalness in itself determines neither the safety nor the moral value of something.
historicalContext: Roots in 19th-century Romanticism and Rousseau's 'noble savage'. Today a pillar of organic product marketing, supplements, alternative medicine, and the anti-vaccine movement. Also used to justify social conservatism ('it's against nature').
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the evolutionary distrust of the new and unknown, and the association between nature and purity. 'Green' marketing has reinforced this bias: 'natural' has automatically become synonymous with 'safe', 'healthy', 'better' — regardless of reality.

variants.name: Natural marketing
variants.desc: '100% natural ingredients' = automatically healthy

variants.name: Alternative medicine
variants.desc: 'Natural remedies' vs 'chemical pharmaceutical poisons'

variants.name: Naturalistic conservatism
variants.desc: 'It's against nature' to justify social norms

variants.name: Anti-technology
variants.desc: 'Non-GMO food is healthier' without specific evidence

redFlags: 'Natural' used as automatic synonym for 'safe' or 'healthy'
'Artificial' or 'chemical' used as insults without specific context
Lack of specific evidence, only reference to naturalness
Social arguments based on 'it's against nature'

caseStudies.title: Arsenic and penicillin
caseStudies.description: Arsenic is perfectly natural and is a deadly poison. Penicillin is industrially produced and has saved hundreds of millions of lives. Nature is neither good nor bad: it is indifferent.
caseStudies.lesson: Naturalness does not determine safety or efficacy. Every substance must be evaluated for its specific properties, not its origin.

defense.action: 'Natural' is not proof: what specific scientific evidence supports the claim?
Natural poisons, life-saving synthetic medicines: naturalness is irrelevant
Evaluate demonstrated properties, not origin

scenario.title: Dietary supplement
scenario.situation: An ad: 'Our supplement is 100% natural, no artificial chemical ingredients. Choose real health.' No study cited.
scenario.question: What unproven assumption is the logical foundation of this advertising message?

options.text: That 'natural' automatically means 'safe and effective' — a correlation that does not exist in chemistry or pharmacology
options.explanation: The most potent poison known (botulinum toxin) is 100% natural. Arsenic is natural. The correlation 'natural = safe' has no scientific basis but a very strong intuitive basis — which the message exploits.

options.text: That the company has not conducted studies: perhaps it has but does not cite them due to space constraints
options.explanation: If favourable studies existed, the message would use them — it is the most effective way to sell a supplement. The absence of citation is a signal, not an uncertainty to resolve charitably.

options.text: That consumers do not already know that natural ingredients are preferable
options.explanation: This answer accepts the premise that natural ingredients are preferable — which is exactly the assumption to be questioned.

name: Slippery Slope
subtitle: The Slippery Slope Fallacy
summary: Claiming that a small action will inevitably cause a chain of extreme negative events, without demonstrating the causal link.
definition: The slippery slope is a logical fallacy asserting that a single action will inevitably lead to a cascading series of negative consequences — increasingly severe — without demonstrating why each step is inevitable. It is used to block any change, even reasonable ones, by raising extreme scenarios.
historicalContext: Used systematically against social reforms: civil rights, women's suffrage, marriage equality — each step was presented as the first stone in a civilizational collapse. Also used in war rhetoric and legal conservatism.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the pessimism bias and fear of the unknown. The human mind tends to extrapolate linearly: if A happens, B will happen. The argument doesn't require proof for each step — it just needs to evoke vivid enough catastrophic scenarios.

variants.name: Moral
variants.desc: 'If we legalize X, then they'll want to legalize Y and then Z'

variants.name: Political
variants.desc: 'This law will lead us to totalitarianism'

variants.name: Technological
variants.desc: 'If we use this technology, we'll lose control of everything'

variants.name: Irreversible chain
variants.desc: 'Once started it can never be stopped'

redFlags: Consequences presented as inevitable without proof
Undemonstrated logical jumps between A and the final catastrophe
The final scenario is extreme and very distant from the initial proposal
No consideration of intermediate measures or possibility of correction

caseStudies.title: Arguments against women's suffrage
caseStudies.description: Women's right to vote was opposed also with slippery slope arguments: it would dissolve the family, then morality, then civilization. None of that happened.
caseStudies.lesson: History shows that reforms don't automatically generate the predicted catastrophes. Evaluate each step for its specific demonstrable consequences.

defense.step: Ask for proof of each step
defense.action: 'Why does A necessarily lead to B? Where is the demonstration?'
'Are there mechanisms that would prevent progression toward the extreme scenario?'
Judge the concrete action, not the hypothetical catastrophic scenario

scenario.title: Data regulation
scenario.situation: Proposed data privacy law. Opponent: 'If we give the government access to this data, then it will want everything. Then we'll be a surveillance dictatorship like North Korea.'
scenario.question: What is missing from the opponent's argument to make it logically valid?

options.text: Demonstration of the causal mechanisms that lead from A (data privacy law) through B, C, D to Z (a dictatorship like North Korea)
options.explanation: A slippery slope can describe a real sequence — but only if every step is demonstrated. The argument jumps from 'data access' to 'total dictatorship' without explaining any intermediate steps.

options.text: Nothing: history shows that surveillance laws often expand over time
options.explanation: This is a valid argument about the tendency of surveillance laws to expand — but North Korea is a qualitative leap that would require a specific causal chain, not a generic pattern.

options.text: A concrete example of a dictatorship that arose specifically from data privacy laws
options.explanation: An example would help, but is not sufficient: historical examples do not prove logical necessity. What is missing is the causal chain explaining why this specific law would produce this specific result.

name: False Equivalence
subtitle: Both Sides Are the Same
summary: Presenting two positions as equivalent and equally valid when there are substantial differences in evidence, moral weight, or factual basis.
definition: False equivalence is a fallacy that treats two positions or events as morally, logically, or factually equivalent, ignoring substantial differences in the quality of evidence, moral weight, or context. It produces a false neutrality that actually advantages the weaker or more extreme position.
historicalContext: The foundation of 'false balance journalism': giving equal space to scientific consensus and fringe positions creates the impression of a non-existent debate. Used to equate movements, violences, or responsibilities of radically different intensity.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the desire for fairness and neutrality. The brain tends to see the 'middle way' as moderate and wise. Balanced presentation is perceived as objective, even when the scales have enormously different weights.

variants.name: False media balance
variants.desc: Equal space for experts and denialists as if they were peers

variants.name: Both sides
variants.desc: Equating violences or responsibilities of vastly different intensity

variants.name: Moral equivalence
variants.desc: 'All politicians are the same', 'all ideologies lead to the same result'

variants.name: False analogies
variants.desc: Superficially similar but structurally different comparisons

redFlags: Comparison between positions with radically different evidence bases
Presenting scientific minorities as equally valid to the consensus
Moral equation of actions of very different severity
'There are two sides' used to avoid fact-based judgments

caseStudies.title: Climate change in the media
caseStudies.description: For years, media presented the climate debate as '97% of scientists vs. some skeptics' with equal airtime, creating the perception of a non-existent open scientific debate.
caseStudies.lesson: Journalistic balance doesn't require equating positions with radically different evidence. Objectivity concerns facts, not formal symmetry.

defense.action: 'How much evidence supports each position? Are they comparable?'
Identify the relevant differences that the false equivalence hides
Neutrality doesn't mean giving equal weight to all positions

scenario.title: Vaccine debate
scenario.situation: A talk show features two guests: a virologist with 30 years of research and an anti-vaccine influencer. The host: 'There are two legitimate positions on this topic, let's hear both.'
scenario.question: Why does presenting two guests with opposing positions not constitute a journalistically balanced format?

options.text: Real balance requires weight proportional to available evidence — not equal time between positions with incomparable epistemic foundations
options.explanation: Giving equal space to two positions creates in the audience the impression that they are equivalent. This is a distortion opposite to partisanship: equation where no equivalence exists.

options.text: In a democracy every position deserves to be heard: the format is correct
options.explanation: Listening is different from equating. A scientifically balanced format does not give equal space to flat-earthism and geography — even though both are 'positions'.

options.text: It depends on the quality of the influencer's arguments: if they are valid, the format is justified
options.explanation: The question is not to evaluate this specific influencer: it is that the format structurally creates equivalence before any arguments are presented.

name: Scapegoating
subtitle: The Scapegoat
summary: Attributing blame, problems, or crises to a specific group in order to distract from real causes and channel collective frustration.
definition: Scapegoating is the practice of attributing responsibility for complex problems, crises, or failures to an identifiable group — ethnic, social, professional, religious — artificially simplifying causes, distracting from real analysis, and creating a unifying target for collective frustration.
historicalContext: An ancient practice (the 'scapegoat' in the Bible). The central mechanism of every genocide and persecution: Jews in Nazi Germany, kulaks in the USSR, Tutsi in Rwanda. Used systematically in economic crises to divert popular anger toward minorities.
psychologicalMechanism: The brain seeks simple causes for complex problems (simple causality). A scapegoat provides: an understandable cause, an identifiable enemy, an illusorily simple solution ('eliminate the problem by eliminating them'), and a sense of cohesion in the 'we' group against 'them'.

variants.name: Ethnic/religious
variants.desc: Minorities as the cause of economic or social crises

variants.name: Professional
variants.desc: 'The bankers', 'the journalists', 'the politicians' as cause of all ills

variants.name: Geographic
variants.desc: 'Europe', 'the capital', 'the North' as responsible for everything

variants.name: Ideological
variants.desc: 'The communists', 'the liberals' as universal explanation

redFlags: Complex problems with a single cause identified in one group
The accused group is already minority or vulnerable
The 'solution' consists of eliminating or excluding the group
The narrative intensifies during periods of economic or social crisis

caseStudies.title: Weimar and hyperinflation
caseStudies.description: The German hyperinflation of 1923 had complex structural causes (war reparations, monetary policies). Nazi propaganda attributed it to Jews, creating the scapegoat that would fuel the Holocaust.
caseStudies.lesson: Real economic crises have complex structural causes. Those who offer a simple, identifiable culprit are almost always distorting reality.

defense.action: 'What specific evidence proves this group is responsible?'
Complex social problems almost always have multiple structural causes
Does the accusation increase during crises? Who benefits politically from this scapegoat?

scenario.title: Economic crisis
scenario.situation: A leader: 'Unemployment is the fault of immigrants who steal jobs from citizens. Before them this problem didn't exist.'
scenario.question: Why is this explanation of unemployment politically useful but analytically weak?

options.text: It converts a complex structural problem — with multiple causes over decades — into a visible, identifiable, and attackable target
options.explanation: Simplicity is politically effective: a concrete enemy mobilises; structural causes do not. But simplicity comes at the cost of accuracy — and policies that do not address the real causes.

options.text: Because it does not cite economic sources: with supporting data, the argument might hold
options.explanation: Data on the impact of immigration on the labour market are contradictory and highly context-dependent. The absence of sources is a problem, but not the only or primary one.

options.text: Because it is a debated opinion in economics: experts disagree
options.explanation: Economists' disagreement concerns the magnitude of effects, not the simplified mechanism presented here. The problem is the reduction of a complex system to a scapegoat.

name: DARVO
subtitle: Deny, Attack, Reverse
summary: When confronted with a behavior, the manipulator denies it, attacks the person making the accusation, and presents themselves as the true victim.
definition: DARVO (Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender) is a sequence of manipulative responses: first the contested behavior is denied, then the person reporting it is attacked by discrediting them, and finally roles are reversed by presenting oneself as the true victim. Recognized by psychologist Jennifer Freyd as a typical response of abusers and institutions when confronted with accountability.
historicalContext: Theorized by Jennifer Freyd in 1997 in the study of trauma and abuse. Recognized as a systematic institutional response in scandals (Church abuse, sports violence, corporate harassment) and in dynamics of power abuse.
psychologicalMechanism: Reverses the burden of proof and transfers attention from the act to the motivation of the person reporting it. Observers tend to perceive the counter-accusation as plausible (documented DARVO effect: observers tend to consider victims less credible after a DARVO response).

variants.name: Institutional
variants.desc: Organizations that attack those who report internal abuse

variants.name: Relational
variants.desc: Partners who present themselves as victims after causing harm

variants.name: Political
variants.desc: Accused who turn the accusation into political persecution

variants.name: SLAPP
variants.desc: Strategic lawsuits to intimidate and silence those who report

redFlags: A response to criticism that never contains a response to the actual criticism
The accused systematically becomes the victim after the accusation
Attacks on the credibility or motives of the person reporting
The report is presented as the aggression, not the reported behavior

caseStudies.title: Institutional scandals
caseStudies.description: Institutional DARVO is documented in major scandals: deny the accusations, attack the witnesses as 'liars with hidden agendas', then claim the institution is the true victim of a defamatory campaign.
caseStudies.lesson: The DARVO response does not refute the content of the accusation. Always ask: 'What is the response to the specific contested behavior?'

defense.action: Deny + Attack + Reverse = DARVO. Recognizing the sequence neutralizes the effect.
'You're not responding to the specific accusation. What is your response to X?'
Being reported for a behavior is not the same as being persecuted

scenario.title: Workplace complaint
scenario.situation: An employee reports harassment. The manager responds: 'Nothing happened. She has been persecuting me for months with false accusations. I am the victim of a campaign to ruin my career.'
scenario.question: In this response, in what order do the three manipulative strategies appear?

options.text: First denies the facts ('nothing happened'), then attacks the credibility of the person reporting ('she has been persecuting me'), then presents himself as the victim ('I am the one being victimised')
options.explanation: The order is structurally necessary: the denial is the foundation, the attack creates the alternative frame, the victim-perpetrator inversion is the result. Reversing the order would make the move less effective.

options.text: Attack first, then denial: the denial is more effective after destabilising the accuser
options.explanation: In this specific case the denial comes first. But above all: the question asks you to recognise the structure of the response, not to hypothesise alternative sequences.

options.text: There are not three separate strategies: it is a single understandable emotional response from someone who feels unjustly accused
options.explanation: This interpretation, if accepted, is exactly the intended effect of the move. The tripartite structure is documented and systematic, not a chaotic emotional reaction.

name: Overton Window
subtitle: Shifting the Bounds of the Sayable
summary: Gradually making previously unthinkable ideas acceptable by progressively shifting the boundaries of public debate toward extreme positions.
definition: The Overton Window describes the spectrum of ideas politically acceptable at a given moment. By shifting it gradually — through proposing extreme ideas, then less extreme ones, then acceptable ones — it becomes possible to normalize what was once unthinkable. The mechanism also works involuntarily: those who propose extreme positions shift the center toward themselves.
historicalContext: Theorized by Joseph Overton in the 1990s for the Mackinac Center. Today central to the analysis of populist and extremist movements: gradually introducing radical proposals until they are normalized in public discourse.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits perceptual adaptation and the anchoring mechanism: repeatedly exposed to extreme positions, the brain redefines the 'center'. The mere discussion of an idea — even to refute it — partially normalizes it by making it less taboo.

variants.name: Shift toward the extreme
variants.desc: Progressive proposal of increasingly radical positions

variants.name: Involuntary Overton
variants.desc: Media normalize extreme positions simply by discussing them

variants.name: Accelerated window
variants.desc: Crises used to make exceptional measures acceptable in a short time

variants.name: Debate anchoring
variants.desc: Extreme positions that shift 'the reasonable' toward themselves

redFlags: Proposals unthinkable a decade ago now discussed as reasonable
Moderate positions increasingly resembling yesterday's extreme positions
Simply discussing an idea presents it as a legitimate option
Crises used to introduce 'temporary' measures that become permanent

caseStudies.title: Normalization of extremism
caseStudies.description: Historians of extremist movements document how proposals initially rejected as unthinkable become gradually normalized: first discussed, then considered, then implemented.
caseStudies.lesson: Monitoring the window's shift over time is more important than judging individual proposals at the moment they appear.

defense.action: 'Was this position acceptable 10 years ago? What has changed in the debate, not in the facts?'
Choose how and when to discuss extreme positions: discussion itself can shift the window

defense.step: Identify who benefits from the shift
defense.action: 'Who benefits from this proposal now being considered reasonable?'

scenario.title: Political discourse over time
scenario.situation: Proposals that 15 years ago were considered extreme by all parties now regularly appear in mainstream debate as options 'worth considering'. No specific event has made them more valid.
scenario.question: What distinguishes this shift from normal political debate evolution?

options.text: The absence of new arguments or events justifying the change — the boundary has shifted through repeated exposure, not through reasoning
options.explanation: Legitimate debate evolution follows new evidence or new collective experiences. Here the change occurred through progressive normalisation: repetition has made familiar what was extreme, without anything justifying it.

options.text: It is normal evolution: political positions change over time in all democratic systems
options.explanation: Evolution exists — but here the cause is missing. When positions change without a change in evidence or reality, normalisation is the most probable mechanism.

options.text: The fact that mainstream parties have adopted these positions certifies their normalisation
options.explanation: Institutional normalisation is an effect, not a certification of legitimacy. Mainstream parties adopt extreme positions when the ground has already been prepared — they are not the judges of their acceptability.

name: Spiral of Silence
subtitle: The Silenced Majority
summary: Those who perceive their opinion as a minority tend to stay silent, artificially amplifying the visibility of dominant positions.
definition: The spiral of silence (Elisabeth Noelle-Neumann, 1974) describes the process by which people who believe their opinion is in the minority tend not to express it publicly for fear of social isolation. This silence further amplifies the visibility of the dominant position, creating a spiral: dissenters perceive their isolation as growing and silence themselves further.
historicalContext: Theorized by Elisabeth Noelle-Neumann in 1974. Consciously used in propaganda to create the perception of unanimous non-existent consensus. Social media has intensified the mechanism: digital silence is more visible and measurable.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits the evolutionary need for group belonging. The brain constantly monitors the 'climate of opinion' to avoid social isolation — historically a survival threat. The perception of being in the minority activates the self-censorship mechanism.

variants.name: Pluralistic ignorance
variants.desc: Everyone privately thinks X but believes they are the only one — no one says it

variants.name: Silence of moderates
variants.desc: Extreme positions are louder; moderates stay quiet

variants.name: Perceived algorithmic censorship
variants.desc: The perception (even if mistaken) of censorship produces real self-censorship

variants.name: Constructed silent majority
variants.desc: Artificial amplification of a position to make it seem dominant

redFlags: Feeling of being 'the only one' who thinks differently without real verification
Consensus perceived as unanimous on topics that historically divide people
Environments in which expressing dissent carries high social costs
Media presenting a position as 'obviously shared by everyone'

caseStudies.title: Propaganda of unanimous consent
caseStudies.description: Authoritarian regimes use the spiral of silence systematically: showing enthusiastic crowds, eliminating any expression of dissent, making people believe that support is unanimous. Those who dissent believe themselves isolated and stay silent.
caseStudies.lesson: Silence is not consent. The visibility of an opinion does not correspond to its actual prevalence.

defense.action: 'Does the consensus I perceive match surveys, data, or private conversations?'
A loud opinion is not necessarily a majority opinion
Private conversations often reveal hidden dissent that the spiral mechanism conceals

scenario.title: Work environment
scenario.situation: In a meeting, management presents a controversial decision. No one objects. Afterwards, talking with colleagues individually, you discover that almost everyone was against it but thought they were the only ones.
scenario.question: If almost everyone was opposed, why did no one speak up in the meeting?

options.text: Everyone used others' silence as a signal of agreement — reinforcing an impression of majority that did not exist
options.explanation: The mechanism is circular: your silence convinces others you agree, their silence convinces you that you are the only one opposed. The 'majority' was a collective illusion produced by fear of isolation.

options.text: Corporate hierarchy inhibits dissent: it is the pressure from the boss, not the perception of isolation
options.explanation: Hierarchy contributes, but does not explain why afterwards everyone believed they were alone. Hierarchical pressure creates silence; the spiral creates the belief that everyone agrees.

options.text: They were genuinely in agreement in the meeting — in individual conversations they changed their minds to please the interlocutor
options.explanation: This interpretation would require all individual conversations to produce the same change, which is statistically implausible. The consistent pattern indicates a systematic mechanism, not a change of mind.

name: Moving the Goalposts
subtitle: Shifting the Standards
summary: Changing the criteria of proof or success every time they are met, to avoid admitting being wrong.
definition: Moving the goalposts is an argumentative fallacy in which, after a required proof or condition is satisfied, the criteria are immediately changed or raised to avoid acknowledging the validity of the opposing argument. It is a form of intellectual dishonesty that makes any evidence-based debate impossible.
historicalContext: Present in all debate contexts: scientific, political, legal, personal. Particularly documented in scientific denialism, where each new piece of evidence is rejected with a new, higher demand for proof — never achievable.
psychologicalMechanism: Exploits debate asymmetry: whoever moves the goalposts never has to provide their own proof, only keep demanding more proof from the opponent. Protects against cognitive dissonance by avoiding admitting being wrong, at the cost of intellectual honesty.

variants.name: Proof escalation
variants.desc: 'One study isn't enough, I want a hundred' (after providing one study)

variants.name: Criteria change
variants.desc: 'That's not what I meant when I said X — now the criterion is Y'

variants.name: Scientific denialism
variants.desc: Every new piece of evidence is met with new unachievable standards

variants.name: Political
variants.desc: Promises redefined after failure: 'That's not what I meant'

redFlags: Every piece of evidence provided is immediately followed by a higher new demand
The criteria change after being met, not before
The goalpost-mover never provides their own criteria in advance
The goal seems to be endless debate, not verification

caseStudies.title: Climate change denialism
caseStudies.description: First: 'There's no evidence'. Then: 'One study isn't enough'. Then: 'The consensus isn't unanimous'. Then: 'The models are uncertain'. The goalposts shift every time the previous criterion is satisfied.
caseStudies.lesson: Establish the criteria for proof before starting the debate. If they change afterward, it's moving the goalposts.

defense.action: 'What would convince you? Establish it before, not after'
Keep track of which criteria had been accepted and when they changed
'That was not the original criterion. You're moving the goalposts'

scenario.title: Scientific debate
scenario.situation: First: 'I need evidence.' You provide one study. 'One study isn't enough.' You provide ten studies. 'They could be funded by interested parties.' You provide independent studies. 'The samples are too small...'
scenario.question: What does the continuous shifting of criteria in this sequence reveal about the position of the person demanding proof?

options.text: That the objective is not to reach truth but to maintain indefinitely the position of scepticism, regardless of the evidence provided
options.explanation: The shifting criteria is the key signal: if the problem were the quality of evidence, new criteria would be proposed in advance, not each time existing ones are met.

options.text: That the scientific standard has risen over time: it is normal that stronger evidence is required today than in the past
options.explanation: Standards can rise — but they do so transparently and uniformly, not ad hoc every time a specific piece of evidence is provided. The pattern here is reactive, not progressive.

options.text: That the studies provided genuinely had limitations, which justifies further requests
options.explanation: Every study may have limitations — but if limitations are identified only after presentation, and if requests change with each response, the pattern is not critical: it is evasive.

name: Negging
subtitle: The Compliment That Hurts
summary: Using veiled criticisms or negative compliments to destabilize someone's self-esteem and create dependency on the manipulator's approval.
definition: Negging is a manipulative technique consisting of making ambiguous comments, veiled criticisms, or 'compliments' that contain an implicit negative evaluation. The goal is to lower the target's self-esteem just enough to make them insecure and in need of the manipulator's approval, creating psychological dependency.
historicalContext: Codified in PUA (pickup artist) communities of the 2000s as a seduction technique. Now recognized by psychologists as a technique of emotional abuse and coercive control in relationships, not only romantic but also professional and familial.
psychologicalMechanism: Destabilizes the target's self-evaluation system by making the manipulator's judgment the reference point. Those who receive negging enter a cycle of approval-seeking: they try to prove their value to the person who diminished them, increasing dependency on that person's judgment.

variants.name: Compliment-criticism
variants.desc: 'You're attractive for your standards' — inserts an implicit hierarchy

variants.name: Destabilizing observation
variants.desc: 'You seem insecure when you speak in public' said unsolicited

variants.name: Diminishing comparison
variants.desc: 'The other person did that better than you'

variants.name: Criticism disguised as concern
variants.desc: 'I'm telling you this for your own good: you're not up to it'

redFlags: Unsolicited comments about your adequacy, appearance, or competence
Compliments that always contain an implicit reservation or unfavorable comparison
Recurring feeling of never being 'enough' with that person
Attempts to regain approval by changing your behavior

caseStudies.title: Emotional abuse in relationships
caseStudies.description: Negging is documented as an early phase in narcissistic abusive relationships: first love bombing creates dependency, then systematic negging lowers self-esteem to consolidate control.
caseStudies.lesson: Comments that systematically lower your self-esteem are not 'honesty' or 'concern' — they are control.

defense.action: 'That comment seemed critical rather than constructive — was that your intention?'
Your value doesn't depend on the approval of someone who diminishes you
A single comment may be a mistake; a systematic pattern is manipulation

scenario.title: Professional environment
scenario.situation: Your manager often says: 'Good work, for what you can do.' 'You've improved, considering where you started.' Every week a similar comment. You find yourself working harder and harder to prove your value to them.
scenario.question: Why does this type of feedback push you to work harder instead of making you feel valued?

options.text: Partial approval generates a cycle in which you constantly seek full recognition that never arrives — and the manager maintains control over your motivation
options.explanation: This is the mechanism of intermittent reinforcement: non-guaranteed but possible approval is more motivating (and more dependency-inducing) than consistent approval. Control over approval becomes control over the person.

options.text: Because you are ambitious and want to improve professionally — the feedback stimulates you
options.explanation: Genuine ambition responds to clear and achievable standards. Here the standards shift systematically: every improvement is minimised instead of recognised.

options.text: Because your manager has high standards and you want to meet them — it is a relationship of professional esteem
options.explanation: High standards are communicated with clear objectives and specific feedback. The described pattern does not provide criteria for improvement — it provides permanent demonstration of partial inadequacy.

name: Emotional Contagion
subtitle: The viral spread of collective emotions
summary: Exploits the human tendency to synchronise one's emotions with those of surrounding people, creating collective moods that favour the reception of propagandistic messages.
definition: Emotional contagion is the phenomenon by which emotions are transmitted from person to person almost automatically, often without critical awareness. In propaganda, it is deliberately cultivated through mass gatherings, evocative imagery and narratives that trigger uniform emotional responses in large groups.
historicalContext: Nazi party rallies at Nuremberg were engineered to maximise emotional contagion: lighting, symbols, marches and rhythmic speeches synchronised crowd reactions. Elias Canetti in 'Crowds and Power' (1960) analysed how emotional fusion within a mass abolishes individual judgement.
psychologicalMechanism: Mirror neurons predispose us to mimic others' emotional expressions. In an emotionally homogeneous and engaged group, the critical threshold drops: others' emotions become our compass for reality, supplanting rational evaluation.

variants.name: Social media contagion
variants.desc: Viral posts and videos that amplify collective emotions globally within hours.

variants.name: Crowd effect
variants.desc: Emotional intensity increases proportionally to the size and homogeneity of the group.

variants.name: Music and rhythm
variants.desc: Anthems, chants and synchronised rhythms induce shared emotional states and a sense of unity.

redFlags: Communications designed to stir strong emotions before presenting facts
Images or stories eliciting fear, anger or euphoria without context
Sense of emotional urgency that prevents reflection
Language that describes collective emotions ('we are all outraged')

caseStudies.title: Social media and 'outrage storms'
caseStudies.description: Studies on viral diffusion show that content with high negative emotional charge spreads up to six times faster than neutral content, creating waves of collective indignation often based on partial information.
caseStudies.lesson: The speed of emotional propagation is not correlated with the truthfulness of the information.

caseStudies.title: American political conventions
caseStudies.description: US presidential conventions are designed by event management experts to maximise emotional intensity: lighting, music, speech timing and crowd reactions are choreographed to create televised emotional contagion.
caseStudies.lesson: An orchestrated emotion is not an authentic evaluation.

defense.action: Ask yourself: was this emotion deliberately induced? Who benefits from it?
Step away from the emotionally charged context before forming definitive judgements.
Check information on independent sources, away from the emotional atmosphere.
Knowing that emotional contagion exists is the first step to limiting its influence.

scenario.title: The march of anger
scenario.situation: You attend a large public rally. Dramatic music, relentless speeches and thousands of angry people make you feel a growing indignation towards a specific group. After the rally, you hear a friend say: 'I finally understand how dangerous they are!'
scenario.question: What best describes the psychological mechanism at work?

options.text: Your friend processed new information that rationally changed their view.
options.explanation: The change happened in a highly emotional context, not through an informational evaluation.

options.text: The collective emotional intensity lowered individual critical thresholds, synchronising the group's reactions.
options.explanation: Correct: emotional contagion in a mass suppresses individual judgement. The emotion perceived as collective appears to 'confirm' a fact.

options.text: The rally provided convincing statistics justifying concern.
options.explanation: The effectiveness of the rally relies precisely on emotion, not data.

options.text: Your friend used critical thinking to reassess their beliefs.
options.explanation: A high-emotion context is the opposite of conditions favourable to critical thinking.

name: Smokescreen
subtitle: Diverting attention from real problems
summary: Technique that consists of deliberately creating informational noise — sensational news, minor scandals or artificial controversies — to divert public attention from uncomfortable or damaging issues.
definition: A smokescreen (also known as a 'red herring') is the art of shifting the public agenda towards an alternative topic when the current one is unfavourable. It can be intentional (launching a distraction story) or structural (media systems that privilege spectacle).
historicalContext: The term derives from the military use of smoke grenades to conceal troop movements. In politics, the classic example is the 'foreign war' used to distract from domestic opposition. During the Watergate scandal, Nixon attempted to divert attention with foreign policy announcements. In the 21st century, social media have made this technique ubiquitous.
psychologicalMechanism: Cognitive attention is a limited resource. When overwhelmed with information about topic X, we have less capacity to process topic Y. The smokescreen exploits this limitation: it does not deny the problem, it renders it irrelevant through attention saturation.

variants.name: Whataboutism
variants.desc: Responding to criticism with 'but what about you/them?' to shift the debate.

variants.name: Strategic news bomb
variants.desc: Launching a sensational story (true or false) to eclipse another unfavourable one.

variants.name: Information overload
variants.desc: Producing so many stories in a short time that none receives adequate attention.

redFlags: Important announcements arriving at politically convenient moments
Minor scandals exploding when major issues are being discussed
Responses to criticism with 'but others also...'
Stories that disappear before being resolved, replaced by new urgencies

caseStudies.title: The distraction tweet cycle
caseStudies.description: Analysis of political leaders' tweets has shown statistically significant correlations between posting provocative content and the occurrence of unfavourable news at the same time, suggesting deliberate use of the mechanism.
caseStudies.lesson: Ask yourself: does this story arrive at a convenient moment for someone?

caseStudies.title: Whataboutism in the Cold War
caseStudies.description: The USSR perfected whataboutism as a diplomatic tool: to every criticism of human rights violations, it responded with references to American racial segregation. The technique has returned in contemporary propaganda.
caseStudies.lesson: A 'they also' does not address the original criticism: it avoids it.

defense.action: Note the original question and verify whether the response actually addresses it.
Ask yourself whether the new story emerges at a convenient moment for someone.
A story can be urgent (new) without being more important than the one it distracts from.
Actively choose which topics to follow, rather than passively following the flow.

scenario.title: The providential announcement
scenario.situation: A minister is at the centre of a corruption inquiry that monopolises newspaper headlines. The day before an explosive report is due to be published, the government announces a major anti-drug police operation with a live press conference and spectacular images. The report receives little coverage.
scenario.question: Which mechanism best describes what happened?

options.text: The media chose the story most relevant to the public.
options.explanation: The relevance of the two stories was comparable; the choice was influenced by the convenience of the timing.

options.text: A spectacular story was used to saturate media attention and reduce coverage of an unfavourable one.
options.explanation: This is the smokescreen mechanism: the information is not denied, it is rendered invisible through competition for attention.

options.text: The anti-drug operation shows the government is working well despite the inquiry.
options.explanation: The two matters are independent; a good action does not cancel out a potentially bad one.

options.text: The public has shown a preference for public safety news over political news.
options.explanation: This is not a public preference, but the result of editorial choices influenced by the timing of the announcement.

name: Self-Fulfilling Prophecy
subtitle: When predictions create the reality they describe
summary: Mechanism by which a belief or prediction, once disseminated, induces behaviours that make it come true. In propaganda, it is used to create panic, instability or polarisation through narratives about inevitable future events.
definition: Theorised by sociologist Robert K. Merton in 1948, the self-fulfilling prophecy describes how a false definition of a situation evokes behaviour that makes the originally false definition come true. In propaganda, it is exploited to create the conditions of crisis that were supposedly being prevented, or to justify authoritarian measures.
historicalContext: Bank runs are the classic example: the rumour that a bank is in difficulty (even if false) provokes mass withdrawals that put it genuinely in difficulty. In geopolitics, predictions of inevitable conflict between nations fuel arms races that make conflict more likely. Carl Schmitt theorised how defining the enemy creates the enemy.
psychologicalMechanism: When we believe an event is probable, we change behaviour to prepare for or prevent it — and often these changes contribute to causing the event. Propaganda exploits this mechanism by creating narratives of 'inevitability' that lead to collective mobilisation towards a predicted scenario.

variants.name: Moral panic
variants.desc: Demonisation of a social group that provokes rejection reactions confirming the stereotype.

variants.name: Arms race spiral
variants.desc: Predicting an enemy attack justifies armament, which the other interprets as a threat and arms in turn.

variants.name: Decline prophecy
variants.desc: Narratives of inevitable economic or cultural decline that demotivate investment and accelerate decline itself.

redFlags: Predictions of 'inevitability' used to justify emergency measures
Narratives that present future conflicts as certain rather than as risks
Definitions of groups or nations as intrinsically dangerous
Use of deterministic language ('It is only a matter of time', 'It is inevitable')

caseStudies.title: Bank runs and the 2008 crisis
caseStudies.description: During the financial crisis, the perception that some banks were insolvent (even before they were) prompted investors and depositors to withdraw capital, making genuinely insolvent institutions that were in difficulty but not yet in crisis. The narrative of crisis accelerated the crisis itself.
caseStudies.lesson: Financial narratives do not merely describe reality: they construct it.

caseStudies.title: The 'clash of civilisations' prophecy
caseStudies.description: Samuel Huntington's 1993 thesis on the 'clash of civilisations' was used by political actors to frame international relations in terms of inevitable conflict between Islam and the West, helping create the political and cultural conditions that made such a clash more likely.
caseStudies.lesson: Presenting a risk as certainty is not analysis: it is propaganda that constructs the reality it describes.

defense.action: A future event is always conditional. Ask what conditions would make the prediction false.
Who makes the prediction and what do they gain if it comes true?

defense.step: Evaluate the effect of the narrative itself
defense.action: Ask: does spreading this prediction increase or decrease the probability of the event?

defense.step: Seek alternatives to the deterministic narrative
defense.action: Every scenario presented as inevitable has alternative scenarios that the prophets do not mention.

scenario.title: The dangerous neighbour
scenario.situation: A politician repeatedly claims that an ethnic minority in the country is 'inherently prone to crime' and that an increase in their presence will 'inevitably' lead to increased violence. Many citizens begin treating members of that group with suspicion. Crime statistics remain unchanged, but social tensions rise.
scenario.question: What effect is the politician's narrative producing?

options.text: They are informing the public about a real risk documented by statistics.
options.explanation: The data do not support the claim; the narrative creates a perception disconnected from reality.

options.text: They are creating the social conditions (suspicion, tensions) that could generate the conflicts described as inevitable.
options.explanation: Correct: defining a group as dangerous alters social behaviour towards that group, creating real tensions from a false premise.

options.text: They are exercising their right to free speech without concrete effects.
options.explanation: Political narratives have documented behavioural effects; they are not without consequences.

options.text: They are accurately describing a demographic trend.
options.explanation: Crime statistics remain unchanged: the narrative is disconnected from empirical reality.
